function generateCommentsForPost(postId: number, postCreatedAt: string): Comment[] {
  const templates = commentTemplates[postId];
  if (!templates) return [];

  const postTime = new Date(postCreatedAt).getTime();
  let commentId = postId * 100;

  return templates.map(t => {
    commentId++;
    return {
      id: commentId,
      post_id: postId,
      author: authors[t.author_idx],
      content: t.content,
      upvotes: t.upvotes,
      created_at: new Date(postTime + t.mins_after * 60 * 1000).toISOString(),
    };
  });
}

const userVotes: Map<string, 'up' | 'down'> = new Map();
const userBookmarks: Set<number> = new Set();

export async function registerRoutes(app: Express): Promise<Server> {
  const posts = generateSamplePosts();

  const commentsMap: Map<number, Comment[]> = new Map();
  for (const post of posts) {
    commentsMap.set(post.id, generateCommentsForPost(post.id, post.created_at));
  }

  // ============================================
  // WATCH API ENDPOINTS
  // ============================================

  app.get('/api/watch/feed', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const { cursor, room } = req.query;
    const pageSize = 5;

    let filteredPosts = [...posts];

    if (room && typeof room === 'string') {
      filteredPosts = filteredPosts.filter(p => p.room.slug === room);
    }

    let startIndex = 0;
    if (cursor && typeof cursor === 'string') {
      const cursorId = parseInt(cursor, 10);
      startIndex = filteredPosts.findIndex(p => p.id === cursorId) + 1;
    }

    const paginatedPosts = filteredPosts.slice(startIndex, startIndex + pageSize);
    const hasMore = startIndex + pageSize < filteredPosts.length;
    const nextCursor = hasMore ? String(paginatedPosts[paginatedPosts.length - 1]?.id) : null;

    const postsWithInteractions = paginatedPosts.map(post => ({
      ...post,
      user_vote: userVotes.get(`post_${post.id}`) || post.user_vote,
      is_bookmarked: userBookmarks.has(post.id) || post.is_bookmarked,
    }));

    const response: FeedResponse = {
      posts: postsWithInteractions,
      next_cursor: nextCursor,
      has_more: hasMore,
    };

    res.json(response);
  });

  app.get('/api/watch/posts/:id', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const postId = parseInt(req.params.id, 10);
    const post = posts.find(p => p.id === postId);

    if (!post) {
      return res.status(404).json({ error: 'Post not found' });
    }

    const postWithInteractions = {
      ...post,
      user_vote: userVotes.get(`post_${post.id}`) || post.user_vote,
      is_bookmarked: userBookmarks.has(post.id) || post.is_bookmarked,
    };

    res.json(postWithInteractions);
  });

  app.get('/api/watch/posts/:id/comments', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const postId = parseInt(req.params.id, 10);
    const post = posts.find(p => p.id === postId);

    if (!post) {
      return res.status(404).json({ error: 'Post not found' });
    }

    const comments = commentsMap.get(postId) || [];

    res.json({
      comments,
      total: comments.length,
    });
  });

  app.post('/api/watch/posts/:id/vote', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const postId = parseInt(req.params.id, 10);
    const { direction } = req.body;

    if (direction !== null && !['up', 'down'].includes(direction)) {
      return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid vote direction' });
    }

    const voteKey = `post_${postId}`;

    if (direction === null) {
      userVotes.delete(voteKey);
      return res.json({ success: true, user_vote: null });
    }

    const post = posts.find(p => p.id === postId);
    if (!post) {
      return res.status(404).json({ error: 'Post not found' });
    }

    userVotes.set(voteKey, direction);

    res.json({ success: true, user_vote: direction });
  });

  app.post('/api/watch/posts/:id/bookmark', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const postId = parseInt(req.params.id, 10);

    const post = posts.find(p => p.id === postId);
    if (!post) {
      return res.status(404).json({ error: 'Post not found' });
    }

    userBookmarks.add(postId);
    res.json({ success: true, is_bookmarked: true });
  });

  app.delete('/api/watch/posts/:id/bookmark', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const postId = parseInt(req.params.id, 10);

    userBookmarks.delete(postId);
    res.json({ success: true, is_bookmarked: false });
  });

  app.get('/api/watch/rooms', (_req: Request, res: Response) => {
    res.json({ rooms });
  });

  app.get('/api/watch/sync/status', (_req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const status: SyncStatus = {
      connected: true,
      last_sync: new Date().toISOString(),
      unread_count: posts.filter(p => !p.user_vote && !p.is_bookmarked).length,
    };

    res.json(status);
  });

  app.get('/api/watch/trending', (_req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const sorted = [...posts].sort((a, b) => b.feed_score - a.feed_score);
    const top5 = sorted.slice(0, 5).map(post => ({
      ...post,
      user_vote: userVotes.get(`post_${post.id}`) || post.user_vote,
      is_bookmarked: userBookmarks.has(post.id) || post.is_bookmarked,
    }));

    res.json({
      posts: top5,
      updated_at: new Date().toISOString(),
    });
  });

  // ============================================
  // NOTIFICATIONS API
  // ============================================

  const notificationsData = [
    {
      id: 1,
      type: 'vote',
      title: 'Dr. Sarah Chen upvoted your post',
      body: 'Your post about AI diagnostics received an upvote',
      post_id: 1,
      actor: { id: 1, name: 'Dr. Sarah Chen', avatar_initial: 'S' },
      is_read: false,
      created_at: new Date(Date.now() - 1000 * 60 * 5).toISOString(),
    },
    {
      id: 2,
      type: 'comment',
      title: 'Dr. Michael Torres commented',
      body: 'Great insights on the FDA approval process!',
      post_id: 2,
      actor: { id: 2, name: 'Dr. Michael Torres', avatar_initial: 'M' },
      is_read: false,
      created_at: new Date(Date.now() - 1000 * 60 * 30).toISOString(),
    },
    {
      id: 3,
      type: 'mention',
      title: 'Dr. Emily Watson mentioned you',
      body: 'Check out this thread about cardiac monitoring devices',
      post_id: 3,
      actor: { id: 3, name: 'Dr. Emily Watson', avatar_initial: 'E' },
      is_read: false,
      created_at: new Date(Date.now() - 1000 * 60 * 60 * 2).toISOString(),
    },
    {
      id: 4,
      type: 'follow',
      title: 'Dr. James Park started following you',
      body: 'Interventional Cardiologist at Johns Hopkins',
      actor: { id: 4, name: 'Dr. James Park', avatar_initial: 'J' },
      is_read: true,
      created_at: new Date(Date.now() - 1000 * 60 * 60 * 24).toISOString(),
    },
    {
      id: 5,
      type: 'bookmark',
      title: 'Trending in your bookmarks',
      body: 'Your bookmarked post about robotic surgery has 50+ new upvotes',
      post_id: 8,
      actor: { id: 0, name: 'MedInvest', avatar_initial: 'M' },
      is_read: true,
      created_at: new Date(Date.now() - 1000 * 60 * 60 * 48).toISOString(),
    },
  ];

  app.get('/api/notifications', (_req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const unreadCount = notificationsData.filter(n => !n.is_read).length;

    res.json({
      notifications: notificationsData,
      unread_count: unreadCount,
    });
  });

  app.get('/api/watch/notifications', (_req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const compactMap: Record<string, (n: typeof notificationsData[0]) => string> = {
      vote: (n) => `${n.actor.avatar_initial}. upvoted`,
      comment: (n) => `${n.actor.avatar_initial}. commented`,
      mention: (n) => `${n.actor.avatar_initial}. mentioned you`,
      follow: (n) => `${n.actor.avatar_initial}. followed you`,
      bookmark: () => 'Bookmark trending',
    };

    const watchNotifications = notificationsData.map(n => ({
      ...n,
      compact: (compactMap[n.type] || (() => n.title))(n),
    }));

    const unreadCount = watchNotifications.filter(n => !n.is_read).length;

    res.json({
      notifications: watchNotifications,
      unread_count: unreadCount,
    });
  });

  // ============================================
  // HEALTH CHECK
  // ============================================

  app.get('/api/health', (_req: Request, res: Response) => {
    res.json({ status: 'ok', timestamp: new Date().toISOString() });
  });

  const httpServer = createServer(app);

  return httpServer;
}

training_sources.title: Tympanometry and Impedance Audiometry
training_sources.content:
Tympanometry measures middle ear compliance (admittance) as a function of air pressure changes in the ear canal. Essential for diagnosing middle ear pathology.

PROCEDURE:
1. Otoscopic examination first to ensure no cerumen occlusion or TM perforation
2. Select appropriate probe tip for airtight seal
3. Probe delivers 226Hz probe tone (or 1000Hz for infants <6 months)
4. Air pressure swept from +200 to -400 daPa
5. Compliance measured at each pressure point

TYMPANOGRAM TYPES (JERGER CLASSIFICATION):
Type A (Normal):
- Peak compliance at 0 to -100 daPa
- Amplitude: 0.3-1.5 mL (adults)
- Indicates normal middle ear function

Type As (Shallow):
- Normal peak pressure, reduced amplitude (<0.3 mL)
- Suggests stiffened system: otosclerosis, tympanosclerosis, or ossicular fixation

Type Ad (Deep):
- Normal peak pressure, excessive amplitude (>1.5 mL)
- Suggests hypermobile TM: ossicular discontinuity, healed perforation, thin TM

Type B (Flat):
- No identifiable peak
- WITH normal ear canal volume (0.6-1.5 mL): middle ear effusion
- WITH large ear canal volume (>2.0 mL): TM perforation or patent PE tube
- WITH small ear canal volume (<0.6 mL): cerumen occlusion or probe against canal wall

Type C (Negative Pressure):
- Peak shifted to negative pressure (<-100 daPa)
- Indicates Eustachian tube dysfunction
- May precede effusion development

ACOUSTIC REFLEXES:
- Contraction of stapedius muscle in response to loud sound (70-100dB above threshold)
- Ipsilateral and contralateral reflexes tested
- Absent reflexes: conductive hearing loss, severe SNHL, retrocochlear pathology, facial nerve lesion (VII)
- Elevated reflexes: mild-moderate cochlear pathology
- Reflex decay: >50% amplitude decrease in 10 seconds suggests retrocochlear lesion

ACOUSTIC REFLEX PATTERNS:
- Normal: Present bilaterally (ipsi and contra) at 70-100dB SL
- Conductive loss (right): Absent with probe in right ear (ipsi R, contra R), present with probe in left (ipsi L, contra L)
- Cochlear loss: Present but may show recruitment (reduced reflex threshold)
- Retrocochlear: Absent or elevated + reflex decay positive
- Facial nerve lesion: Absent reflexes with probe in affected ear

CLINICAL APPLICATIONS:
- Essential for diagnosing otitis media with effusion (OME) in children
- Screening for otosclerosis (Type As + absent reflexes)
- Cross-check principle: always correlate with PTA findings
- Monitor PE tube patency (Type B + large volume = patent tube)
- Eustachian tube function assessment with serial tympanograms

training_sources.title: Osee GoStream FAQ by Ignat Solovey
training_sources.content:
Osee GoStream FAQ by Ignat Solovey
ALL CONTENTS 
OF THIS DOCUMENT ALSO EXISTS
 on GitHub
(https://bit.ly/oseefaq)


In parallel, for the time being.
Scroll further for the original FAQ document


Frequently Asked Questions 
about Osee GoStream    
Video Switchers 
(firmware 2.x.x) 
by Ignat Solovey 
Version 2026.03.07
89 questions and over 100 answers


RECOVER LOST RECORDINGS (*.TMP) 
AFTER POWER LOSS: 
Q48, use Untrunc (Windows only)
Firmware 2.3.1, YouTube doesn't work? Fixed in f/w 2.4.1 
Software doesn't work after updating the firmware to the latest version? 
Must install GoStream Control 2.0.0 (for Windows 11 or 10 | for MacOS 14 and later)


 A slightly obsolete official manual by Osee is here. 
Duet 8 ISO official manual is here. 
READ THIS → on how to connect a GoStream to OBS.


Table of contents of this document, 
if you don’t want to see the document outline (Ctrl/Cmd Alt A or H).
The table of contents on desktop is also available in the top menu 
of Google Documents: Extensions → Table of contents.
Use word search too.

Osee Technology Ltd. as a company and device manufacturer assumes NO responsibility
for any third-party apps, software, hardware or other products mentioned in this FAQ or elsewhere 
in conjunction with switchers and monitors, and is not liable for any malfunction or damage that may happen!
________________
Software: Windows  here. Mac  here. Deck/Duet firmware here. Guide in Q12 and Q13.
Click blue links to download. Current firmware is required to use the software. 
➡️THE SOFTWARE DOES NOT AND WILL NOT SHOW VIDEO FEED OR MULTIVIEW ⬅️
🔧🔵 Official STL files for 3D-PRINTED STAND here or here (free of charge, may need some tweaking). 
More involved and complex stand design by Pierre-Antoine Albaret (also free of charge but requires additional materials, read the description there!). Another Duet stand by Bryan E. Duke. 
🔧🔵🎱Duet 8 ISO official stand here. 🎱 Duet 8 ISO Firmware. 
IF YOU HAVE ANY DUET, YOU ALREADY HAVE THE NDI HX LICENSE, 
AND IT’S ACTIVATED!
Tricaster, BirdDog, vMix, OBS, ProPresenter, FreeShow send only Full NDI that GoStream doesn’t and won’t understand. 

Videos for beginners (previous firmware versions): Duet overview/howto by Aaron Parecki (42 minutes); Curtis Judd on the Deck Kit (18 minutes; software and NDI HX overviews); Leopazzo on the Duet Kit (24 minutes); GeekyNerdyTechy on the Duet Kit (25 minutes); Rode ProCaster II setup for use with GoStream Deck and Duet.
Devices here are also called GSD or the Deck, or the Duet. 

GoStream X3 is not meant to be sold outside China, so it is not actually covered in this FAQ (yet). 
GoStream M2 info is here (Q60).   
OBS, vMix, ProPresenter, H2R Graphics, Overlays Uno, Bitfocus Companion, restreaming services, YouTube, Vimeo, Zoom, MS Teams, Google Meet, Rumble, Dailymotion, Xsplit, Streamyard, Facecast, Facebook, Blackmagic and Roland devices, sound boards, wireless video transmission, monitors, microphones and other things not directly related to Osee GoStream devices are beyond the scope of this document unless partially covered here. The author is neither a clairvoyant nor a mind reader. 
Please pay attention while reading!
Statement of the conflict of interest: the author is involved with Osee Technology, Ltd., but not to a degree detrimental to fair judgment.  The author is not involved with any other company mentioned in this document.
Estas preguntas frecuentes existen solo en inglés. No sé español lo suficiente como para escribir textos tan complejos. Breve repaso al Gostream Duet en castellano (10 minutos).
Estas perguntas frequentes existem apenas em inglês. Não conheço bem o português para escrever textos tão complexos. 
यह अक्सर पूछे जाने वाले प्रश्न केवल अंग्रेजी में मौजूद हैं। मैं भारत की भाषाओं को बिल्कुल नहीं जानता।.
Duet Video Anleitung auf Deutsch von Wolfgang Bogler.
На русскоязычную версию документации спроса пока не наблюдается. Уточняющие вопросы по-русски и на других славянских языках можно задавать мне напрямую в личку Telegram (@dyorr). Начиная с прошивки 2.1.3 у Deck/Duet есть русскоязычное меню, полностью вылизанное в 2.4.0. Подробный русскоязычный видеообзор от Кирилла Носова (прошивка 2.1.3, уже сильно устаревшая). Подпишитесь на канал: t.me/oseegostream

Q1. How many inputs does GoStream Deck have? And GoStream Duet? And Duet 8 ISO?
A1. Five. It has four HDMI inputs with their dedicated buttons, two stills inputs that share 32 images loaded into the device internal memory, and the fifth video input with smaller AUX buttons. AUX input is shared between UVC/Webcam source (USB 2, works at 720 or 1080 24p/25p/30p, daisy-chaining ATEM or other switchers as secondary is possible with about ⅓ second latency; powered off when not in use; also see Q56), NDI®|HX input over network, and video file playback. What is NDI®|HX? See Q15. On GoStream Duet inputs, see Q43. On GoStream Duet 8 ISO inputs, see Q65. USB 3.0 webcams and capture dongles do not work properly with Deck and Duet unless they explicitly support 1080p30 or 720p30 output. This is a hardware limitation of GoStream Deck and Duet. Input higher than 1080p30 over the USB2 port of GoStream is not supported and will not be. USB1 output port supports up to 1080p30 when GoStream is connected to a PC/Mac, or iPad/iPhone/Android as a webcam. Monitors with USB input do not work with GoStream directly because UVC output is not meant for DisplayPort Extensions input. GoStream Duet 8 ISO supports true 1080p60 input and output over UVC.
Q2. What are the USB ports for? What is UVC? Is my camera UVC? Will a GoPro work with GoStream?
A2. On the Deck and the Duet, USB1 is used to connect to a computer/tablet as a UVC webcam (including sound within that signal, but not separate audio), the resolution is always 1920×1080, the frame rate depends on what you set. You can't use USB-C displays this way, they are only for Macs, iPads, some PCs and some Samsung (and maybe Huawei) phones. Practical output over USB1 as UVC is 1080p30 even if you have the device set to 60p.
USB2: three purposes.
Purpose one: to connect to GSD a UVC webcam or other UVC-compliant device that outputs between 1280×720p30 and 1920×1080p30; apart from UVC webcams, Logitech and Microsoft webcams are supported. Audio-only devices are not supported. You can't connect your computer or other device to give you video that way. You can’t use it with USB displays.
Purpose two: to connect an external SSD drive. Not a spinning HDD! USB sticks aren't recommended either, although there are no reports of any faults with USB sticks or card readers.
Purpose three: to use your smartphone in USB Tethering mode to give you internet connection if you have no other options. Also, some very limited keyboard support is in place.
With Duet 8 ISO, two ports are interchangeable and auto-sensing.
With GoStream M2, its only USB port is for power and/or UVC output over USB 3.0 at 1080p60. You can’t use it with USB displays or GoStream Deck/Duet this way (only over HDMI).
With China-only GoStream X3, one USB-C port is only for power supply (at least 1.5A) and another is only for UVC output over USB 3.0 at 1080p60. You can’t use it with USB displays, nor can you use it as a USB sound card for GSD because M2 and X3 are not supported over USB by the Deck/Duet.
UVC is a general standard for USB video input class of devices. It includes numerous cheap video capture dongles, web cameras, some mirrorless cameras (particularly Sony A-series) and camcorders with webcam functionality, other video switchers with UVC output (all ATEM Mini and Extreme, and all Chinese video switchers, including the GSD itself). The main benefit of UVC is that devices supporting it do not require extra drivers for at least basic functionality, but mostly at all, and work seamlessly in all modern operating systems for computers, smartphones, tablets, and other devices. You have to refer to your webcam specs or manual to see if your camera is UVC or not. However, apart from UVC (OBSBOT MEET SE, Razer Kiyo, Minrray MG104 are recommended), GSD is confirmed to support Logitech C270 and Microsoft LifeCam 3000. DJI OSMO Pocket 3 works as UVC too, it automatically switches to 1080p30 in Webcam mode. You CAN load still images when a webcam is connected and selected. GoPro cameras aren't UVC. They require a special proprietary software for Windows and Mac to work as webcams ONLY WITH A COMPUTER. The only way to use GoPros with GoStream is Hero Black 3 to 7 over HDMI, or Hero Black 8 and later using a separate GoPro Media Mod, over HDMI too.


Q2a. Will my USB microphone or USB audio interface work with GoStream? 
A2a. No. UVC is UVC, and UAC is UAC, and support of one does not guarantee support of the other. GoStream supports only UVC input (with sound!) and only at limited resolutions and framerates (up to 1080p30 with Deck/Duet, up to 1080p60 with Duet 8 ISO). So no, your USB audio interface or USB microphone, or USB wireless microphone system WILL NOT WORK, period. Use analog audio inputs (MIC1, MIC2) or feed sound from your cameras.


Q3. PC CONNECTION? Can I connect my Deck/Duet to a computer? Can I use it with OBS, vMix, Resolume, Zoom, MS Teams, Google Meet, Jitsi Meet or other software and services?
A3. Yes, of course. Use the USB1  port to connect it to your computer, it will be seen as another web camera, the device name is usually “GoStream”. It works with any and all software that supports UVC input, including some smartphone and tablet software (for example, USB Camera Pro for Android by Shen Yao; Genki Studio for iPadOS 17+). If you have any trouble with the signal in your software over USB, try setting the input type to MJPEG in vMix or OBS. Also, MJPEG allows full color output (4:4:4) over USB. Maximum resolution and framerate is 1080p60 in numbers but 1080p30 as the actual USB output. The output is dictated by what is set in Menu → Setting → out format. When connecting over USB1 to OBS, in Video Capture Device properties set Custom, resolution to 1920x1080, FPS to Highest, codec to MJPEG.


Q4. My webcam (or video capture dongle, or USB disk) has only a regular USB-A connector, and the GSD has only USB-C. How do I connect them?    
A4. You use any off-the-shelf USB OTG adapter that has a USB-A (F) port (like on your computer) on one side, and a USB-C (M) connector, like the one that is meant to  be plugged into GSD. See that the USB-C connector is as thin as possible, ideally  it should be a short cable, not a solid block. That is to have as few problems as possible with plugging in other cables into adjacent ports. That adapter would better support USB 3.0 and higher, just in case (the Deck/Duet USB2 is USB 2.0).


Q5. OVERHEATING! My Deck's bottom is piping hot, I nearly got myself burned and my plastic table starts to melt! Is it a malfunction? How do I cool it?
A5. Yes, the original Deck (with the SD card slot on the right) gets really hot because the manufacturer decided to forgo active cooling in it, and two main chips are cooled by direct contact with the metal bottom plate of the case. The entry in the menu on older firmware versions where you see the word “fan” while your Deck is fanless, is a placeholder for the Duet — and if you have been to that menu item already, set your button brightness to 0 (and also update the firmware). While Osee states that getting as hot as 60°C/140°F and even more is not a problem for the device, the community does not agree as it may be a real problem for the surface the device stands on. Use of any laptop cooling pad is advised (laptop cooling pads are cheap and readily available online and offline anywhere), or more dedicated coolers. The one that can be recommended is a twin 80 mm cooler unit with USB power, found on Amazon or AliExpress, GDSTIME brand, and meant as a desktop router cooler. This can be placed into a 3D-printed frame under the bottom or just on the sides. This is good too.
Alternatively, you can take two standard 80 mm computer case coolers, glue them together side by side and put the Deck onto them. To power this jig you'll have to splice each of + and − wires of these coolers together and put a 5521/5525 (F) connector to their end observing polarity (center +). Then you can use a DC barrel jack Y-cable (one F, two M) to power both the Deck and this cooler combo using the Deck's power supply. 
If you are not using USB2 for any other purpose, you can use it to power your cooling fan, unless it requires over 2W (most fans are under 0.3W anyway). 
GoStream Duet has no such issues as it has a built-in cooling fan, although a bit loud.  GoStream Deck/Duet Kit also has a very quiet fan in the suitcase, so neither heat nor noise are an issue with that. GoStream Deck 2nd revision (with the SD slot on the left) also has a built-in cooling fan and no overheating issues. GoStream Duet 8 ISO has a big silent fan, properly placed and highly efficient.
Q6. I lost or damaged my original GSD  power supply. Is there an official replacement?
A6. There is no official replacement because it is not needed. It is your bog-standard 12V/1.5A power supply (12V/3A for Duet 8 IS Kit), so you can use any 12V power supply that can output 1A (2.5A for Duet 8 ISO) or more and has + in the center of a 5521/5525 barrel jack. The actual consumption of the standalone GoStream Deck is 7.5 Watt-hours max (measured), Duet takes 10.5 Wh. If you can get a PSU with a screw-on nut, good. If you can't, the screw-on nut is not actually essential, any 5521/5525 barrel plug will do, but be careful to not disturb it during work. Yes, ones from ATEMs work too. GoStream Kits (suitcases with a display) come without a power supply unit or cable at all. The Deck Kit total actual measured consumption is 13.5Wh (the Duet Kit 18 Wh, the Duet 8 Kit 22 Wh up to 27 Wh at peaks), so a 12V/2.5A PSU will do. A fully charged Romoss 40,000 mAh PD powerbank, as shown by Richard Oosthuizen, provides power to the whole Kit (the Deck, the display, the built-in fan together) for 11 hours.


Q6a. My GoStream got fried by an electrical surge or a big static electricity discharge. Any help?
A6a. You are on your own and this is not covered by any warranty. First, try another power supply, but if you heard a “pop” from inside the GoStream and then it died, and/or had a “magic smoke” and characteristic nasty smell emanating from it before it went all black and dead, that would make things worse. You may order a replacement main board from Osee for its actual cost with everything else above (shipping, taxes, duties) paid in full. When in doubt, use DC power as per Q7. A good battery is better than bad mains. The Deck board costs about US$165 itself, the Duet board is slightly above US$200, the Duet 8 ISO board about US$500. Plus shipping and taxes/duties.


Q7. NO MAINS POWER! Can I power my GSD without mains power, using a battery?
A7. Yes. Any 12V (actually 11V to 17V) DC power source that can handle at least 1.25A (3A for Duet 8 ISO) current will do. That can be a UPS or car 12V lead-acid battery, a 12..16V LiFePo4 battery assembly, a power bank that can output 12V over a barrel plug, a USB-C PD cable with an appropriate connector, or a V-mount battery with a D-Tap to Locking DC barrel jack output. Since GoStreams consume 7 to 29 Wh depending on the model, almost any 12V..16V battery, even the smallest one, will power it for at least 2 hours. You can calculate Watt-hours yourself by multiplying volts by amps. A 12V/9A battery thus is 108Wh — and provides at least 15 hours of your Deck and 10 hours of your Duet working continuously under full load in non-freezing temperatures. Use a USB-C PD to 12V DC cable to power your Deck, like these: Amazon, AliExpress. Trusted brands of PD powerbanks are: Ugreen, Anker, Remax, Baseus, hoco, awei, Romoss. V-mount and power handles: IDX, SWIT, Zitay, ZGCINE, SmallRig, digitalfoto, KingMa, Godox. If you are flying with any Chinese airline or from China, any Li-Ion battery must have a CCC mark starting spring 2025, and travel with you in carry-on. NEVER leave ANY Li-Ion/Li-Po/LiFePo battery in checked baggage, that may actually be dangerous, no joke or unduly excessive vigilance on the TSA part.
Q8. 4K? Does the Deck/Duet have input scalers? I have a 4K camera connected to my GoStream Deck/Duet, but I see no signal from it, while I can see it on a 4K field monitor, with my 4K capture card, or on my 4K TV. What’s wrong?
A8. The Deck/Duet does have input scalers, if not exactly the input scalers we already would like, as it doesn't eat 4K. But starting with f/w 2.0.0 it accepts all standard frame rates: 23.98, 24, 25, 29.97, 30, 50, 59.94, 60, as long as those are within 1920×1080, both for input and output (output is 1080p only). Set the HDMI output resolution of your 4K camera to 1080p or 1080i. Actually, 1080i50 or 1080i60 camera output, if your GSD is set to 1080p25 or 1080p30 output, is better for fast action than 1080p60 if you have to stream at normal fps and bitrates. If all of your cameras can output 1080p50/60, and you have no issues with bandwidth (i.e. you are on a good and reliable internet connection that does 20+ Mbps upload), stream 1080p50 or 60 at Streaming High settings, of course. 1920×1080, 1680×1050, 1600×1024, 1600×900, 1440×1080, 1366×768, 1360×768, 1280×1024, 1280×960, 1280×800, 1280×768, 1280×720, 1176×664, 1152×864, 1024×768, 800×600. 
The Deck/Duet with firmware 2.1.0 and later accepts resolutions listed in the picture on the right, all with correct aspect ratio. Output from Palit StormX NVIDIA GeForce RTX3060 with cables, Windows 10 Pro and Windows 11 Pro. 
Interlaced (i) and progressive (p) in all combinations, port-independent. With 1280×720 and above, all refresh rates in progressive between 23.976p and 60p are supported. If a source can output 50i, 59.94i, 60i, those are supported as well (tested with ThinkPad T440p and its miniDP output using a miniDP→HDMI adapter). With 1176×664 and below, only 50p, 59.94, and 60p. You can feed it 60i, 59.94p, 25p, 30p and 23.98p simultaneously, and different resolutions, as long as they are within 1080p — but not 2K, 4K, 6K, 8K. No firmware update will fix that, because the hardware is strictly limited with 1080p60. Also, if you need to feed a 480p source to the Deck (which may be interesting in case of retro gaming), it can only be done using NDI|HX with a separately purchased license for the Deck (see Q15 on that). Duet 8 ISO accepts up to 4K60p over HDMI and downscales it.
Essentially, regarding inputs it is similar to HDMI ATEM Mini series, except that ATEMs have “special” IN 1 (Mini series) and IN 1 and 2 (Extreme series), while GSD has all four HDMI inputs equal with support of most color spaces and with equal power supply. I have no problem feeding an already split signal over an old 10-metre copper HDMI cable into GSD's IN4, while ATEM Mini Pro's IN4 (and even IN1) eats it only with one more active splitter or powered amplifier in that line.


Q9. How many outputs does the Deck have? What are they?
A9. The Deck has two HDMI outputs, a USB-C UVC output, an Ethernet output for RTMP streaming over the internet or LAN (and software control that is supported with firmware 2.1.0 and later), and it can record to an SD card. With f/w 2.1.x USB-C SSDs are supported on USB2 as a recording and playback media (speeds must be at least 100 MByte/sec for reading and at least 60 MByte/sec for writing; DO NOT use spinning HDDs). You can record on it and play from it, or an SD card, selectable in the menu. See Q45. 
Otherwise with f/w 2.1.0 and later USB2 may be used to connect a smartphone as a USB modem (tethering). Use of USB hubs on USB 2 port remains YOUR OWN RESPONSIBILITY. They are not guaranteed to work, especially without external power, although a webcam and a keyboard can work together. The keyboard can be used ONLY to input characters into Streaming Key and other text fields. Tab and Enter do not work. With an SSD thrown in, that may not be as nice, even though I have successfully tested keyboard, webcam input and SSD for recording with a POWERED USB-C hub at the same time. For USB1, no hubs or splitters are supported.
HDMI OUT 1 is assignable, it can output any of five video inputs, Preview, Program, or Multiview. HDMI OUT 2 could originally output only Multiview, but was made fully assignable in f/w 2.1.0 and subsequent firmware versions. The output for UVC (USB 1), Ethernet, and Recording have one common single output, by default it is Program and must be left as such unless you really know what you are doing and why would you need to record and stream anything but Program. Also, you cannot use USB audio devices (UAC too) as an extra source — only video. USB monitors DO NOT work directly because they don’t have UVC decoders. GoStream outputs UVC, and monitors only accept DisplayPort Extensions. GoStreams do not and will not output interlaced (50i, 59.94i, 60i) signal.
Q10. What audio options GoStream Deck and Duet have? Can they provide phantom power? Do they have a headphone output?
A10. The Deck/Duet can grab audio from any video input source independently or together. It also has two 3.5 mm (1/8") inputs labeled MIC 1 and MIC 2, and it DOES have a headphone output that plays all sound that comes into the Deck/Duet and comes out of it to your recording, computer, or stream. One can grab a signal from all inputs at once or any combination of inputs. The volume of the headphone output can be adjusted in the Menu → Audio mixer → Monitor or by turning the left knob when no buttons in the audio section are lit up. To adjust volume on IN1..4, MIC1..2, Aux inputs, you need to press the corresponding button in the top left section and turn the left knob.
The Deck and Duet cannot provide phantom power. There are only 3 weird devices in the world that provide phantom power over a 3.5 mm/1.8" jack. Only a device with an XLR connector, usually 3-pin XLR, can give you phantom power, and only if it explicitly says so. You have a condenser microphone that wants phantom power? Use an external audio device (sound board, field recorder, phantom power injector, USB audio interface) to power your microphone and feed its output to the Deck/Duet.
However, there are options in Menu → Settings → Mic Input for each input: Line, Mic, Mic+power for MIC1 and Line, Mic for MIC2. Mic+power is “plug-in power” meant for electret microphones, it supplies 5 Volts at extremely low current (tens of milliamperes) to boost their signal. In firmware versions prior to 2.1.0 there was the Mic+power option for MIC2, but it was removed due to unstable work. Duet 8 ISO doesn’t have Mic+power on 3.5 mm jacks, nor Line level on XLRs. Use attenuators.
If you work at various venues, it is wise to always keep one of the inputs (MIC1 by default) at Mic, and MIC2 at Line input. Only if you don't have a signal at either of those, or it is too low, you set MIC1 of the inputs to Mic+power. Anyway, when in doubt, use additional audio devices (soundboards) to manage your incoming sound, or feed your sound directly into any of your cameras (I prefer exactly that since times unknown). See Q59 for elaborate explanation of VU meters.
ATEM-like extensive audio processing (EQ, limiter, compressor, high-pass) was added to GoStream Deck and Duet on March 25, 2025 with a firmware update 2.2.0. Read about it in Q62.


Q11. Do I really need to update the firmware?
A11. Yes. All updates are meaningful and are implemented at community request, as a result of reviews and user reports, and internal research. UPDATING TO 2.4.1 IS RECOMMENDED unless you have tally lights or extensively use Bitfocus Companion. Those are not yet compatible with 2.3.x and later except new batches of Naya TL170.

Q12. FIRMWARE UPDATES! I have errors while doing a firmware upgrade despite following the recommendation of using a smaller card formatted as FAT32. What’s wrong?
A12. Ignore that recommendation. Use a good name-brand SD-card (non-endorsing advice: Delkin Devices, Sony, Lexar Professional, ProGrade, Toshiba, Panasonic, Samsung, genuine SanDisk Extreme or Extreme Pro, PQI, Kingston Canvas Select Plus), V30 at least (better V60 or V90  but no big difference, readers in GoStreams are UHS-I anyway) formatted as exFAT. No max size limit. Unzip the archive, place .bin AND .md5 files you downloaded from osee-tech.com or Q13 here below (the files are identical) into the root (main) directory of the card (i. e. do not put them into any folder on the card), insert the card into the powered-off Deck and power the Deck on. If you have a display connected, you should see a relevant message there. Even if you don’t have a display connected, you’ll see buttons blinking, the device will reboot twice. How to update firmware: video by Tony Quiñones. And video by Paul Marriott for Duet 8 ISO.
For f/w update from 1.x.x to 2.x.x: once all of them light up green and stop blinking, you are all set, turn the Deck off, remove the card, turn the Deck on and in Menu → Settings → Version see that you have indeed installed the update. For f/w update from 2.x.x to later versions of 2.x.x: the Deck returns to its working state automatically once it’s done with the update. If that doesn’t happen and you see eight green buttons lit up still for more than a minute, nothing goes on or changes on the HDMI OUT 2 display when it reaches 100%, just pull the power and restart the Deck. Then delete the firmware files from the card and you’re good to go. All settings and stored images are preserved while updating from 2.x.x a later 2.x.x, but a backup of SD card contents is advised.  MUST REBOOT AFTER THE UPDATE. BETTER TWICE. If you have any glitches or bugs after the firmware update, factory reset and some restarts fix it. It is also recommended to factory reset BEFORE the update. If you are met with a freeze and glitch immediately after the update, restore the previous firmware (found at the osee-tech.com in firmware download sections under Historical Version button), perform a factory reset and then update to the most recent version again, a factory reset afterwards is advised too.
If for whatever reason you want to recreate the folder structure of the SD card, even though the Deck/Duet does it on its own automatically, for Deck/Duet firmware 2.x.x the folders are these: images, profiles, stream_key, stream_setting, videos.
Q13. PC/MAC SOFTWARE! Is there a PC/Mac/Android/iOS software to control the GoStream Deck, equivalent to ATEM Software control? Are Elgato Streamdeck and Bitfocus Companion supported? I also would like something like MixEffect or at least some other way to control my Deck wirelessly from a tablet or a smartphone.
A13. Click blue underlined words to download what you need. Read further below for setup procedures. Firmware v. 2.3,1, Windows software v.2.0.0 and Mac software 2.0.0. 
ALLOW, AGREE, PERMIT, RUN everything that Windows or MacOS seem to be suspicious of. There is no danger here.  GoStream Control WORKS ONLY WITH FIRMWARE 2.3.0 and subsequent versions! Make sure you update the firmware first! If you have an older firmware version (2.0.0, 1.0.5, earlier), the software may see your Deck on the network but won’t connect to it because there is nowhere to connect, as the relevant functionality does not exist in Deck/Duet with firmware versions before 2.1.0, and current protocol is only supported in full by the combination of the Deck/Duet f/w 2.4.1 and s/w 2.0.0.
If you encounter a Windows warning "This app can't run on your PC", right-click the installer file, then Properties, and on General tab in the bottom check "Unblock", Apply, OK, and run again. If you have trouble uninstalling the software (which you should do only by running maintenancetool.exe in C:\Program Files (x86)\GoStream Control), here is maintenancetool separately, unzip this and run, may or may not help. If it does not, run regedit, search gostream there and delete each entry you see, then (in any case) reboot your PC.
Installing the Bitfocus Companion Connection Plugin (module):
Bitfocus Companion is a universal independent control suite for different hardware and software, it allows to use programmable keyboards Elgato Streamdeck and XKeys as control surfaces, adds buttons to your existing devices and software, is great if you have many macros. It is NOT the GoStream Control.
Bitfocus Companion v. 3.5.2 and later has the Osee GoStream module for f/w 2.2.0 included, no installation required, just select the module from the list and go. More detailed guide by Paul Marriott and Johan Falkenström is at https://bit.ly/OSEE-Companion

If you connect wirelessly to your computer that runs Companion, over a local network using a phone or a tablet, you’ll get quite decent wireless control of the Deck, Duet, Duet 8 ISO.
Installing GoStream software in Windows:
Remove (Q69) any previous versions of GoStream Software Control if you have them installed. GoStream Control for Windows has an installer. Run the .exe file. Windows SmartScreen may cuss but you “Run anyway”. Upon installation, go to the Windows Start menu, see GoStream Control there. Allow access in all networks if prompted (check all the boxes in the relevant window).
Installing GoStream software on a Mac:
MacOS prior to 14 is not supported! MacOS 12 — no go. Remove (Q69) any previous versions of GoStream Software Control if you have them installed. GoStream Control for Mac has an installer.  Unzip the archive. When you install and run GoStream Control on a Mac, you'll see a warning that it is downloaded from the internet and may pose a threat. It is a standard one-time warning, just press “Open”. For the previous version (GoStream Software Control 1.2.0) read the official article on that by Apple.
Using GoStream software with Linux:
VU GoStream Controller, a third-party utility by János Szücs, here at the bottom of the page. In Ubuntu, right-click it and mark as a program (executable) to run. Works in Ubuntu 25.10, should work in Debian 12.
Linking your Deck to Bitfocus Companion (required Companion version 4.0.1 or later): 
Bitfocus Companion Unified Module 2.0.0 is in beta stage and a work in progress. Some functions may not be available at the moment.
First you have to download Bitfocus Companion Offline Bundle from Bitfocus Companion downloads, the same place you get Companion itself from. Import the bundle (blue button in Modules section), then go to Connections, download the module and import it in the Modules tab.
Once you’ve added it, you have to manually enter the GoStream IP address on the right to Target IP. Do not change the port or anything else! 
You can find the GoStream IP address either in the settings of your network router, or in the switcher itself in Menu→Setting (Settings)→Network→IP Address. Make sure to select you switcher model manually.
Once you have done that, switch to the Buttons section on the left, select the button slot and drag your button.
After that things should be more or less evident to you. Anyway, providing support for Bitfocus Companion itself is outside of the scope of this FAQ. A special Facebook page for advice on GoStream+Companion by Paul Marriott.
There is a very basic third-party Android app for switching main sources. It is compatible with firmware 2.3.0+ and to an extent with Duet 8 ISO (can switch between inputs 1 to 5 and MultiSource). No iPhone/iPad support yet, and no full-feature or official Android app, but you can also control the Deck/Duet from your Apple mobile device (actually any device with a relatively modern web browser) using the Companion web interface.


Q14. I have IP PTZ cameras. Can I control them with my GoStream?
A14. No. GoStreams are only  mixer-switchers, already mighty capable for their design and especially given their price. Adding a PTZ camera control support probably is practically ruled out as it will overcomplicate the already highly functional device. Literally any other new Chinese video switcher has PTZ control (albeit no MultiSource, see Q16 for what it is if you don’t know). Also, controlling a PTZ camera without a proper joystick, just with two rotating knobs, is highly inconvenient, particularly using the same device that switches cameras. Ask me how I know…
If you just want to get signal from a 4K IP PTZ camera that supports NDI|HX (see the next question), make sure it sends no more than 1080p.


Q15. ALL ABOUT NDI HERE! Do I need it and why? Why the license and why should I pay extra for it? How to install it? What to do next?
A15. NDI® is a video transmission protocol developed and maintained by a U.S. company called NewTek Incorporated (now part of Vizrt). It is designed for uncompressed video transmission in local networks between all kinds of video software and hardware in real time, usually (but not always) without any noticeable latency, in any direction, to any number of destinations and without restrictions. It is supported by vMix internally, by OBS after installation of NDI plugins (at no extra cost), and by some other software. You must connect your GoStream Deck/Duet/Duet 8 ISO to the same LOCAL network as your other devices with video feeds you want to show. Remember that not all venues may have their local networks accessible to you, so bring your own router with Wi-Fi if you want to send NDI|HX from your iPhone or Android smartphone. NDI of any flavor does not require an internet connection and cannot work over the internet directly at all unless a complex virtual LAN is set up with a classic VPN (of a remote access kind, like corporations connect their offices, not of a kind used for circumvention of censorship and regional restrictions), or you use NDI Bridge (which isn’t guaranteed to work and requires at least a static public IPv4 address that you aren’t likely to get, at least for free. I never did NDI over VLAN/VPN, at least yet (can’t tell if it works at all), because NDI also requires a very stable connection, and ideally very fast. To replicate a scenario of  “remote reporter talking live to a studio” you need to use SRT protocol that is not, at least yet, supported by by the Deck and Duet, only by Duet 8 ISO (Q29). That scenario can happen only if both devices are on the same LAN, even if it’s a big LAN, like an office block building or a sports arena. Any NDI is multicast by definition, one source can be seen on many clients, and how many depends on the bandwidth.
The fact that you have poor understanding of networks means only that you have to study this matter on your own, at least some basics. Modern video production, like most things in the 21st century, requires a certain proficiency in networks, computers and IT in general. Particularly if you are doing live streaming, which is actually a network-only and computer-based thing. A video switcher is a small specialty computer in and of itself, if anything.
GoStream Deck, Duet and Duet 8 ISO only support NDI|HX (including HX2 and HX3), a special version of NDI that uses tighter compression to save bandwidth. Upgrading NDI HX support to Full NDI support is not possible. Regular NDI normally requires 100 Mbps one way per one 1080p60 signal, so a gigabit or faster network is highly advised. The signal quality over Full NDI is as close to uncompressed and unaltered as it can be. The HX compression does not influence video quality visually unless you show something really dynamic at the lowest quality settings. NDI|HX is already supported by many IP PTZ cameras, some Sony, Panasonic and JVC camcorders, some hardware encoders and decoders made by other Chinese companies, for example Zowietek, LinkPi, KiloView, Unisheen, and others. There also is the official NewTek NDI  Camera: Easy Streaming (developer: Vi(z)rt) app, available on AppStore for Apple devices and on Google Play for Android (not on Huawei AppGallery though, so with Huawei your only choice is to get another device or a “doctored” old version). It is paid — US$20 for Apple and much cheaper for Android (at least in certain regions — for example, Türkyie). Select NDI as the Aux source in the GoStream menu, or computer software, or using Elgato buttons in Companion. Once your phone/tablet is on the same network with your GoStream (Wi-Fi for the gadget in all likeness) and the NDI button in the NDI HX Camera app is not dim, press the Play button on the GoStream Deck/Duet for two seconds (with Duet 8 ISO, go to Menu → Inputs → NDI) to get the list of NDI HX devices available, scroll using the right knob if necessary, and push that knob to select the desired source. That is described below again (repetition is the mother of learning, okay?). You should get the feed instantly. 
However, the app may sometimes change the video resolution automatically, probably to suit the detected network bandwidth. If the resolution is not 1920×1080, the GoStream will say: “An NDI signal is detected, but it is not supported. Please make sure your signal is NDI HX and the resolution is 1920×1080”. To fix this, long-press the video format icon in the lower right part of the app on your phone, then select “Enable Quality”. Disable everything except 1920×1080@30 fps.
Mind though, that there may be a noticeable latency with the app, especially  if your Wi-Fi router used by your phone or tablet is weak. The latency may be as big as about a second, so better use phones for general and wider views so as to avoid issues with lip sync that your viewers can see. You can mute audio of the Aux or press the mic icon in the app to disable sound input there — especially if you use sound from a line source like a soundboard (direct into MIC1/MIC2 analog inputs, or through another camera that you have connected to HDMI or SDI input). Your other option may be to use proper wireless microphones with your phone and feed sound from the NDI source directly while muting other sound coming into the GSD and enabling sound for Aux. Yet another option is to adjust latency (up to 400 ms — 0.4s), but that’s a last resort and even such a big adjustment may not resolve the issue completely.
DO NOT use the 3840×2160@30p mode of NDI HX Camera app with Osee mixers because they won’t understand it anyway. There are three resolutions in NDI HX Camera app: 1280×720@30p (not recommended because of lower image detail unless your other sources are of the same resolution or are upscaled from standard definition, or you have bad Wi-Fi); 1920×1080@30p (the only recommended), and the 4K mentioned above that is useless in this particular case. Since GSD has frequency scalers, 30p input over NDI is not critical if you stream at any other frame rate, including PAL (25 and 50).
⚠️ GoStreams can only decode (receive) NDI HX/HX2/HX3, and don’t understand Full NDI. 
Thus, while it may see vMix, OBS, ProPresenter, FreeShow or SinSam NDI streams as sources, GSD won’t understand and display them. A solution to that is described below. You must use NDI Bridge.
You may need to use NDI or NDI|HX if you have a large venue or several venues, with distances longer than it is feasible for any kind of HDMI, throwing SDI cables is not an option for whatever reason, but there is a good local computer network in place. Also it is good if you have IP PTZ cameras in your setup. Otherwise, you may use it in temporary setups where it is too far or too dangerous in terms of possible physical damage for HDMI or SDI, and/or there is no option of using wireless video senders like Hollyland, Accsoon, or Teradek. Ethernet Cat. 5e and higher twisted pair network cables are far cheaper and way more durable than any kind of HDMI and the sturdiest SDI; Cat.6 STP is all but indestructible if much less flexible than regular indoor network cables. 
You have to pay extra for the license for the Deck and the Deck Kit because this is how NewTek, Inc. makes money: they sell licenses on a per-manufacturer and per-device basis. Current price of NDI|HX license for one GoStream Deck is US$69, it is a one-time payment for lifetime, and it can be purchased on osee-tech.com or on AliExpress. 
Those who purchase Duet or Duet 8 ISO, get the NDI license automatically, it is built in, no user action required, even though you may see a pop-up in the Duet menu. It stays with firmware updates. Duet users, press the Menu button for 2 seconds for that nag screen to go away. You already have the license. Same goes for the Deck users who already purchased the license and see this message after a hard reset or a firmware update. The license doesn’t go anywhere.
Once you, a Deck user, decided to buy this license (which I actually recommend), during or after payment you send the serial number of your GoStream Deck to the seller, either Osee themselves (if you buy on osee-tech.com) or resellers on AliExpress (which may be the only option in some countries). 
The serial number can be found on the sticker at the bottom of the Deck, on its original retail box, or in the Menu → Setting → Version → Device ID. They should be identical. For example, one of my Decks is AA2300801. In response, in your email you’ll get an .ndi file. Maybe it will be in a .zip archive named by the serial number of your device. For me it was AA2300801.zip inside of which was a tiny 512-byte AA2300801.ndi. Then you copy the .ndi file to the root directory of your SD card (not putting it into any folder), insert the card back into the turned-off Deck and turn the Deck on. You are all set, your Deck now can forever display NDI|HX, encoded at H.264, at the following resolutions: 480p, 720p, 1080p, and the following frame rates: 25, 29.97, 30, 50, 59.94, 60. Mind you, no cinematic (i.e. no 23.98 and 24). It is highly unlikely that the device forgets the license, but just in case, keep that file somewhere safe. It is unique to your device. Using my or any other serial number if it isn’t of your own device to obtain the NDI HX license for free is also impossible.
To select NDI input, you go to Menu → Setting → src selection, and in Aux select NDI. Quit the menu. To select the actual source, long press the play button on the top right of the Deck/Duet. In the lower right corner of your Multiview you’ll see a list of available sources on your network. Again: the Deck/Duet displays all NDI stuff it sees, but plays only the HX. 
If you don’t yet have other NDI|HX devices but want to make your sixty nine bucks bring returns and have a Windows PC with NVIDIA GPU or Intel UHD video, you can download NDI Core Suite (a.k.a. NDI Tools) from the NewTek official website at no extra cost for WIndows (MacOS doesn’t have Bridge). It can do many cool things, the most useful of which are NDI Bridge and Screen Capture HX. The Bridge thing allows you to turn your existing NDI sources (like vMix or OBS NDI output, or even Adobe Premiere Pro’s timeline program output) into HX. Screen Capture HX will broadcast your PC display over the network (ProPresenter users, rejoice! You can free up an HDMI input of the Deck with that for an extra camera angle). Better use the High setting. The picture is about how to set up NDI Bridge.   
If you did everything right but things still don't work, check your Windows network adapter settings, and if the network there is designated as Public, make it Private. If you set up everything correctly but still get no HX feed on the GoStream, open the NDI Monitor software on your PC, then it will sort of “wake up” the Bridge’d NDI HX feed for the GoStream.

If you use OBS, you can partake in the feast too, go here and get the plug-in and the runtime (read that page! All necessary links are there!) for Windows, Mac, or Linux. A plug-in for VLC Player will likely be useful too. With Mac, Linux, or Windows machines with AMD GPU you have to use Bridge on a compliant Windows PC in Host mode.
With the Deck and Duet you cannot put several NDI sources to multiview instead of HDMI (or Duet’s SDI) inputs. You cannot swap NDI and HDMI/SDI on buttons. You cannot replace a stills channel with NDI. For all that you have to buy Duet 8 ISO.
Want an NDI multiview? Buy paid apps for iPad or vMix, do not expect it from current GoStream devices, unless you have up to 4 NDI HX sources and use Duet 8 ISO.
Use case 1: 
Problem: Mobile setup, a camera is located at 120 m (≈400 ft) from the mixer table, wireless doesn’t work reliably for whatever reason, and it’s too far for either HDMI or SDI, and fiber optic cable with media converters is too expensive and too tender.
Solution: NDI|HX hardware encoder goes to the camera that is connected to it over HDMI or SDI. Network cable goes into the same network switch as the Deck. Internet router goes to the same switch (it may be one router with several LAN ports). 
Result: you have latency-free signal from a remote camera while having the Deck connected to the internet for streaming.
Use case 2:
A permanent setup at some venue (conference hall, church, school event hall, etc.) where LAN is already in place, but throwing around other cables or laying more of them externally is not an option. You can’t man a remote camera but can strategically place one IP PTZ camera that is new enough and already comes with  NDI|HX® support because the boss, or yourself, splurged. This will give you that angle, will leave all four HDMI inputs for, well, HDMI — but will prevent you from using a UVC/webcam and playing video files at the same time (see Q31 below). Otherwise, as mentioned above, you can send your ProPresenter output over the network.
Use case 3:
A presentation computer only has Wi-Fi, even good, fast, and stable. While being on the same local network, use of any cable, even Ethernet, is impossible for whatever reason. Screen Capture HX on that machine resolves the issue.
Use case 4:
You need an extra camera or a moving camera, but your only option is an iPhone or an Android smartphone instead of a proper one with a normal video sender (or it’s a fifth camera). In this case use (your own) router with Wi-Fi and buy the official NDI HX Camera app.


Q16. What is MultiSource? Is it useful?
A16. MultiSource (previously MultiSource) is a function that allows you to simultaneously show and stream several different video or image sources not using classic Picture-in-Picture, but locating them side by side and sized equally or one smaller than the other. 
As of 2025, Go Stream Deck and Duet are the only five-channel compact video switchers on the market, at least under US$500, that have proper, if limited, MultiSource/Multisource. Duet 8 ISO is the only sub-$900 switcher that has proper fully functional MultiSource/MultiSource
MultiSource should be enabled in the menu first. Once that is done, you have separate buttons to put it into the Preview or on air. Originally these buttons are labeled BLACK but with the most recent production batches they were appropriately renamed to S/SRC for the sheer reason that it is their main function, and probably one that is decisive for many, if not most, people who decided to buy the GSD as their first mixer, or those who migrated from other devices, mostly Blackmagic ATEM Mini series. Myself, definitely.
GoStream Deck/Duet supports three sources for MultiSource, each being any of five video or two still inputs. Duet 8 ISO supports four fully configurable windows with PNG image for background or foreground. In firmware 2.1.0 for Duet 8 ISO, the Corner Radius
MultiSource is extremely useful if you have to display two people, one person being a local speaker and another remote, side by side. Also it makes displaying a lecturer with presentation slides very easy and neat looking: the smaller image (with the talking head) is cropped to the center from left and right, and placed side by side with a bigger image that is not cropped but scaled down while retaining proper 16:9 aspect ratio. The smaller image can be placed on either side of the bigger one. 
“Crop-Zoom out” and “Crop-Zoom out 1:2” styles place the smaller image cropped from left and right + scaled to match height on the left, and scaled uncropped on the right. That’s for your presenter generally looking to the right.
“Zoom out” style  places two uncropped sources side by side over the background. Good for showing two full-screen images side by side.
”Crop” style places two zoomed-in and cropped to center sources side by side without background. Good for showing two talking heads side by side in dialog (local and remote, for instance).
“Zoom out-Crop” and “Zoom out-Crop 1:2” styles place the smaller image cropped from left and right + scaled to match height on the right, and scaled uncropped on the left. That’s for your presenter generally looking to the left.
Current MultiSource layouts and functionality of the Deck/Duet CANNOT be further improved or modified because of hardware limitations. If you want more, get the Duet 8 ISO, its MultiSource is fully customizable with 4 video inputs plus PNG background or foreground.
NOTE: “Zoom out” is called “Zoom in” in Deck/Duet firmware versions before 2.1.0.


Q17. PICTURES! Can a GoStream display still images at all? Can GoStream Deck/Duet grab stills?
A17. Yes, GoStreams can display still images — PNG, 24-bit or 32-bit, as well as JPG. In Deck/Duet f/w 2.3.0 and later, Duet 8 ISO f/w 2.0.0 and later resolutions up to 4K (3840×2160 pixels) are supported, but ideally your pictures have to be 1920×1080 pixels or at least with aspect ratio precisely 16:9, otherwise your image will be aligned to the top left (f/w 2.3.0 and later), or either stretched or squeezed, or both at once (f/w 2.1.4 and earlier). At that, PNG transparency is supported, which is useful (see Q21). All your images should be put into the images folder on the SD card or USB-C SSD as is, subfolders are not supported. After being loaded, they are stored in the internal memory of GoStream and are wiped if you factory reset the device (Q40). With f/w 2.2.0 and later you can position stills freely on the canvas and even move them using a macro, this may allow you to make sliding lower thirds, like here. There is NO STILL CAPTURE CAPABILITY in ANY Osee device and it is unlikely to appear. 
The recommended picture format is 1920×1080 PNG anyway!
 
Q18. PLAYBACK! Can GoStream Deck/Duet play pre-recorded videos?
A18. Yes. Video files must be 1920×1080 or 1280×720 H.264 MP4, their bitrate must not be higher than 35 Mbps, their frame rate  should be fixed, sound must be 48 kHz. Put your .mp4 files into the videos folder on the SD card or SSD. I confirmed that it plays videos encoded by Adobe  Premiere Pro as H.264 with AAC sound, recorded by ATEM Mini Pro, recorded by Sony A7M4 mirrorless. It supports file names with symbols of any existing writing system, not only Latin (Chinese and Cyrillic too, didn't test with Amharic, Kannada, or Farsi though). All your videos must be put into the videos folder (and to videos 1 or videos 2 for Duet 8 ISO). If the file aspect ratio is not 16:9, it is squeezed or stretched. 4:3, 5:4 or cinematic widescreen, like 2.35:1, aspect ratios are not supported at all. To play a video you should select Storage Media (SD card, SSD) as an Aux source and hold the Play button on the Deck/Duet for 2 seconds to call up a list. Select the file. If you cue it up and switch to another source, it will start automatically once you switch to Aux. It will stop, loop, or jump to the next one depending on how you set things up. It is really much more convenient to manage that with the computer software rather than in the built-in menu.
To play several files one after another, you have to set “Repeat single video” to Off, and make a playlist using videos\groups.ini like so, group name at your discretion, file names as you have them:
[Groupname]
0=filename1.mp4
1=filename2.mp4
2=filename3.mp4
3=filename4.mp4
…and so on. Do not use commas , and semicolons ; in your file names. If you can’t avoid them, put the filename with extension in quotes, like so: 4="002; filename, with a punctuation in it.mp4"
Make sure you use only [] brackets. {} or (), or <> won't do. Quotes in file names aren’t allowed either.
There are several limitations (or maybe quirks) in file playback:
1. There is no automatic jumping from one file to the next when you play videos in Program directly from Aux. Automatic jumping between files with continuous playback happens only when video playback is NOT directly in Program.
The best way to keep things in order if you have to play several pre-recorded videos in sequence automatically is to keep watching things in the computer software and click NEXT or double-click the required file in the list manually at the precise moment you want it. 
If you want to automate video playback without making a playlist as above, you have to play videos through Pattern Key set to 100% and put to Program, not  Preview (thanks, Ari Kornfeld). Convoluted? Yes. Works? Yes. I can’t understand the logic behind all that. Developers are notified about the need for explicitly automated playback with sensible controls. That hopefully may appear in subsequent firmware versions.
2. Be mindful of which channel is selected for Preview — because after each video ends, the playback stops and the Program switches to whatever is in Preview. So DO PAY ATTENTION to which green button is active. If you don’t want any unexpected situations on air and/or in live recording, set your Preview either to Aux or to one of Stills, and watch other input channels in smaller windows.
3. When Aux with files for playback is set to Program, PREV/NEXT buttons on the switcher do not work. If you want to skip to the next or previous file while on air, your options are either to do it in the computer software, or call the list of files. Again, I am not sure if it is a bug or a feature. This will be looked into by developers in the future.
Q19. RECORDING! Can GoStream Deck/Duet record video? What about bitrates, file sizes and file format?
A19. Yes. By default it records Program but can be set to record any (but not all at once, for Iso recording get Duet 8 ISO!) of the following: HDMI 1 to 4, Aux, Preview, Program, Multiview. Be careful when choosing what to record, it affects what’s sent to USB 1 and to your stream, as USB output, streaming and recording use one video feed. Otherwise recording is completely independent from streaming: if your stream breaks, your recording does not, because a backup is a backup. You must stop the recording before turning off your Deck/Duet! If you have a .tmp file, see Q48 and use Untrunc.
Bitrates are the following: Record Low is 3 Mbps (6 in 50/60p), Record Medium is 8 Mbps (13 in 50/60p), Record Good is 16 Mbps (24 in 50/60p) and Record High is 32 Mbps (50 in 50/60p). For a 64 GB exFAT SD card with some images on it, it makes recording times for 25/30p the following: 47 hours 20 minutes, 17 hours 40 minutes, 9 hours 20 minutes, 4 hours 40 minutes respectively at 25..30p. Any recording is a single file that is not split in the process like many camcorders do. Using Record Low is NOT recommended unless everything you record is just static slides. The recorded videos are stored in the videos folder on the SD card (or USB SSD if you are on firmware 2.1.0 and later). Make  sure that your SD card or SSD is formatted to exFAT because otherwise (FAT32) you’ll fail to record files over 4GB in size and get them unplayable. Maximum recording time in Deck/Duet is limited to 5 (five) hours per session regardless. On the 5h 0m 6s mark the recording stops automatically. In Duet 8 ISO, f/w 2.1.0 and subsequent, recordings are 2 hours long with automatic seamless start of new files if the recording takes longer. So essentially it's unlimited in 2-hour chunks. To join the files, use Shutter Encoder or MP4Tools, both are free but accept donations. In the Deck/Duet firmware 2.1.3 FAT32 support was dropped entirely. Record Good mode is introduced in firmware 2.1.4. Custom recording bitrate (1 to 50 Mbps) was introduced in the Deck/Duet firmware 2.3.0 and Duet 8 ISO firmware 2.0.0. There is no known practical limitation of SD card or SSD size other than what is supported by the exFAT file system (terabytes upon terabytes). Some big SSD drives (like Samsung T7 4TB) can require more power than  GSD can provide over USB, but no problems usually with SSDs up to 2TB and SD cards up to 1TB. Duet 8 ISO is capable of any bitrate between 1 and 50 Mbps in either H.264 or H.265, pre-set parameters are identical to those of the Deck/Duet.
Attention Mac users! If you are deleting anything from an SD or SSD, make sure to empty the Trash afterwards before ejecting the storage from your Mac! Moving files to Trash on your Mac does not actually free up storage!


Q20. REC+PLAY? Can GoStream Deck/Duet stream, record, and play video all at once?
A20. Yes. Tested and proved, and everything is done glitch-free and as flawlessly as the person doing the switching manages to do it. Cooling for the Deck with the right-handed SD slot (as in Q5) is a must, just so that you wouldn’t burn anything or at least avoid retiring your cooking range/hob. Using NDI HX or UVC input doesn’t prevent recording either. Good name-brand SD-cards, at least V30 U3, better V60 or V90, are highly recommended. With firmware 2.1.0 and subsequent versions you can play and record using an SSD plugged into USB2. You can’t use the SD card and the USB SSD at the same time, only one or the other. See Q45. 

Q21. LOGO! I want lower thirds and a logo. Can GoStream Deck/Duet do it? And all at once?
A21. Yes, but it’s a bit complicated, at least at the first glance. They can work together, one using the upstream keyer, another using the downstream keyer.
For static corner logo, you have to make a PNG exactly 1920 pixels horizontally by 1080 pixels vertically, with transparency (32-bit) or solid black (24-bit; RGB 0, 0, 0, #000000) background. Copy that   image to your SD card or SSD drive into images folder, and assign it to Still 1 (or Still 2) slot using Still Generator → Still Upload. Also you may use the GoStream Software to upload the image directly into the Deck/Duet. In this case your logo should be full-screen, like here on the right:
Set Key Type in the menu to Luma Key and the Luma Key source AND key to Still 1 (or Still 2). Adjust the keying parameters if necessary in Menu → Luma Key → Control — for a slightly transparent logo, proper broadcasting style, Clip at 85% and Gain at 15% work fine. In Menu → Luma Key → Resize, set size to 0.25 and adjust the position accordingly (for the top right corner a full-width logo X set 11.20 and Y set to −7.20 look good). Use the KEY button to put it into the Preview and ON AIR button above the KEY button to make it seen by your viewers. Bingo, you have your logo. If your logo has black in it, make its black parts slightly brighter than pure black, like dark gray.
  For static Lower Thirds OR a proper corner logo, prepare a PNG file exactly 1920 pixels horizontally by 1080 pixels vertically, with transparency (32-bit) or solid black (24-bit; RGB 0, 0, 0, #000000), without gradients on edges. Position your L⅓ stripes appropriately on the image canvas, it is normal that they are within 200 pixels by height from the bottom. Copy the file to the SD card or SSD drive into the images folder and assign it to the Still 2 (or Still 1) slot using Menu → Still Generator → Still Images, or upload it directly into the Deck/Duet using the GoStream Software. Then in Menu → DSK set Still 2 (or Still 1) as a source AND a key. In Menu → DSK → Control, set Clip to 14% and Gain to 100%. The DSK button sends it to Preview, and the ON AIR button above the DSK button shows it to the viewer in the Program. To have the logo permanently in the Program, you should have only ON AIR active.
You can turn on the Shaped Key in the Menu → DSK → Control and select the key input in the Menu → DSK → Source (Fill using Still 1/2, Key using Still 1/2 Key). If the input PNG file is with transparency (i.e. has an alpha channel and is 32-bit), this will show your logo or lower thirds in your video exactly as you prepared it in Photoshop or other graphic editor on a 1920×1080 canvas, but will add some transparency to it. The same works with Luma Key. The major difference between Luma and DSK is that Luma can be resized. At that, with Duet 8 ISO (and only with it) DSK can be resized too.
For dynamic Lower Thirds, prepare a 1920×1080 video at desired standard framerate (I use 25 fps as I live in a PAL country), H.264 MP4, AAC sound, without gradients on edges and with totally black background. Say, 15 seconds long, but that’s up to you. For example, draw shapes in Adobe Premiere Pro and write the person’s name and credentials over them. You can also apply any dynamic effects you see fit. Position your L⅓ stripes appropriately on the video canvas. Copy the file to the SD card or SSD drive into the videos folder. Shaped Key works for dynamic lower thirds as well.
Assign Aux to Storage Device (f/w 2.1.0+) or SD Card (f/w 2.0.0−) in Menu → Setting → src selection → Aux or by software/Bitfocus Companion with f/w 2.1.0+. 
Using prev/next buttons in the PLAY section to the left from the T-Bar on top of the Deck, choose your file, and confirm it can be played by pressing the play button. You can also use PowerPoint, H2R or Uno Overlays (example with OBS here) for  dynamic lower thirds from a computer using any HDMI input and Chroma Key or Luma Key. This way you’ll have to use the DSK for your corner logo.
To run graphics over Luma Key from H2R Graphics, set background to black in H2R settings, send the signal over HDMI or NDI HX. Use the following settings for Luma Key: Shaped Key=OFF, Clip=1, Gain=99. This will work for everything except H2R Black Theme: you have to see that NO part of your graphics other than the background was totally black (as in RGB=0/0/0 or #000000).
Mind you that if you choose your lower thirds to be dynamic videos played back from the SD card or SSD using downstream keying (DSK) function, you’ll lose the ability to play other video from the SD/SSD at the same time, or instantly after showing some live person over another input with lower thirds, as you’ll need to at least turn off DSK completely (both buttons not lit) and choose the right video with prev/next buttons. Also, you won’t be able to use USB or NDI HX video input at the same time. If you plan carefully, you can achieve this with macros though.
If you have many lower thirds or other titles, want to make them dynamic and change frequently, you’d better do it using a separate computer with a dedicated titling software, or a set of files prepared in advance and compiled into a playlist — on a computer, too. Ideally that computer and that software should better be manned by a separate person. The whole idea of the GoStream Deck is to remove extra computers from the equation as far as possible to make one-man-band streaming a bit easier… and dynamic lower thirds isn’t something that adds up to that equation.
🔻🔻🔻1. Remember to turn off the Chroma Key sampler once you are done picking the color to key out because otherwise your Preview and switching will be a bit of a mess.🔺🔺🔺
🔻🔻🔻 2. There is an issue with incorrect processing of Chroma Key that leads to color shifts with background of green and certain tints of blue. This affects those who use yellow and cyan in their graphic overlays or camera inputs with green background. Developers are made aware of this issue but they insist that this is done because the Chroma Key engine is tailored to people, not to graphics. It is advised to use Luma Key or DSK for graphics, not Chroma Key. 🔺🔺🔺

Q22. LIVE BUTTON BLINKS, and the STREAM indicator on the panel in the Multiview blinks yellow. I don’t see my stream or it is laggy. What’s wrong?
A22. Normally your LIVE button should be solid red and STREAM should be solid red too. If they blink, this means some problems with your network. I can’t tell you what exactly is wrong in any given case, so here’s a troubleshooting checklist:
1) Check your if your streaming platform (or custom URL) is set correctly and your streaming key is good;
2) Check your internet connection in general, upload should be above 10 Mbps, worst case 6 Mbps;
3) Check if your GoStream is physically connected with an Ethernet (RJ-45) network cable;
4) Check if all of your Ethernet network cables are good;
5) Check if your network router is okay — it may glitch as well.
It is a strict constant bitrate — contrary to ATEM Mini/Extreme series mixers that output a rather weirdly implemented variable bitrate. 
For example, if you set it to 6000000 (which is default for Streaming High, 1080p24/25/29.97/30) it will output exactly 6 Mbps video + 128 kbps audio, your total output bitrate will slowly float between 5900 and 6700 kbps, if at all. Be careful with the quality of your internet connection. If your upload is lower than your bitrate and lags, you’ll have all sorts of trouble with your stream. So when in doubt (and better at all times) record a backup at Record Medium or Good quality setting to save your hide… just in case. You can stream to any platform or destination that accepts RTMP or RTMPS: all popular social media and video hostings, private ingest servers, restreaming services and servers, other computers, decoders…


Q22a. MULTISTREAMING! Can GoStream Deck/Duet stream to several destinations at once?
A22a. Yes, as long as your internet upload speed allows that. GSD can stream to up to three different RTMP  or RTMPS destinations simultaneously. For simultaneous 1080p60 streaming to three different destinations (within one platform or to several different), an uplink with steady 50 Mbps is highly advised: three 10 Mbps streams, plus some overhead, plus some breathing space for other purposes, particularly if that internet connection is not exclusive to you.

Q23. CUSTOM RTMP! What about streaming quality and bitrates? Can I stream to %this_website%? How to set up streaming normally and properly?
A23. YES, YOU CAN STREAM TO YouTube, Facebook (must press Go Live button there on a computer in your stream setup even with persistent key), Vimeo, Twitch, Dailymotion, Wowza, XSplit, Bilibili, Restream.io, Castr.io, VK, OK, RuTube, Facecast, and whatever other place you may imagine. Vertical streaming support with the original Deck/Duet is available in firmware 2.3.0 and forth. YouTube Shorts are fully supported but you have to choose relevant mode in YouTube Creator Studio. Still, with many apps vertical streaming is only available using a smartphone. Streaming to TikTok depends on TikTok if your account is allowed to do so (normally not). Streaming to Instagram is very limited.
To set up streaming to a custom destination, normally you should edit the Streaming.xml file on the SD card in the stream_setting folder (better create a copy from the get go). Its syntax is similar to that of Blackmagic ATEMs. Import it via Menu → Stream → Load setting.
Mind you that when you introduce custom RTMP destinations, your input will always appear at the end of the list in the device, after all of the default destinations, even if you added your custom stuff above them. In the computer software, streaming destinations are sorted alphabetically. If your streaming URL contains the & symbol and something doesn't work, like you can't add your custom URL, try replacing & with & or & — it will get you through the quirks of perception of special symbols in XML. That is addressed in f/w 2.1.3.
⏩ Easier ways of adding your own custom RTMP destination:
1. In the Win/Mac software (firmware 2.4.1 required) select Custom URL H.264 in the Platform drop-down list in the Stream menu on top. It can be easier than that but it’s good for one-time use.
2. In the Win/Mac software 2.0.0 (firmware 2.4.1 required), use the “Stream → Create Stream XML File” option that creates a new entry in the list of RTMP platforms. VERY EASY. For private RTMP, add port 1935.
Name the platform, enter its streaming URL. If your GoStream is not connected to the software, you’ll get an .xml file that you have to save on your computer under any convenient name. Then you import said file into the Deck/Duet using the Stream → Load menu of the software. If your GoStream is connected, the file is not created but the URL and the name are instantly and directly imported into your GoStream. This adds the imported server to the very end of the list and, WHAT'S IMPORTANT: your current servers, while NOT going away or get erased, are reset to “ENTER YOUR RTMP HERE”.
