Don't let that scare you. After importing a new server URL, you have to manually set your streaming servers in Stream 1/2/3 to what they should be, selecting them from the list.
⏬ The harder way of adding you own custom RTMP destination (it’s Streaming.xml on the screenshot).  
Mind you that there should be no spaces within tags, especially <url>here</url>. 
Again, this is wrong: <url> rtmp://yourstreamingservice.com:1935/live </url>.
This is correct: <url>rtmp://yourstreamingservice.com:1935/live</url>.
You may overwrite the original file or create your own. The file name is not critical, it is not necessarily to name your file exactly Streaming.xml. Can be mystreams.xml, myservers.xml, whatever you fancy.
Once you import your custom streaming settings, you can set up your stream in STREAM1, or STREAM2, or STREAM3. 
In the device menu, YOUR CUSTOM DESTINATIONS ARE IN THE END OF THE LIST.
In the computer software, all streaming destinations are sorted alphabetically.
In the firmware 2.2.0 and earlier, you will always see words ENTER YOUR RTMP NAME HERE in the beginning of the list of streaming destinations.
Make sure you backup xml files with your custom streaming URL destinations because they are wiped from the device memory when they are reset.
There's a bug in Duet 8 ISO firmware 2.0.0 that after shutdown it forgets RTMP added manually to Streaming.xml (while remembering Custom RTMP), which is addressed in 2.1.0.


Q24. How do I get my streaming keys into the Deck/Duet?
A24. If you update the Deck and Duet firmware to 2.1.0 or anything later and install the software control (see the top of this document or Q13 for links), adding streaming keys becomes really easy and can be done on a computer in the Stream section of Software Control on the right. Just paste your  keys in the relevant fields using Ctrl and V together on your keyboard (Command and V on a Mac). 
Deck/Duet Firmware 2.0.0 and later: there is a folder named stream_key on the SD card. Create a .txt file, title it sensibly (my_youtube.txt, church_fbpage.txt, restream.txt, school_2026-02-12_event.txt,  daughter-s_friend-s_twitch.txt are good enough), put your streaming key there in the first line, without spaces or line breaks in front of the first character and after the last one. Save the file. Import your key in Menu → Stream → Stream 1/2/3 → Upload setting. 
If you have nothing at hand that can put a .txt file onto an SD card, not even a phone, and not even a keyboard to connect to USB2 you can punch it in through the menu, using the right knob, one character at a time. Good luck, bro/sis. Best of luck to you… Turn and push, turn and push.
For Duet 8 ISO, it is only software or text file. Manual dial-in does not work.

Q25. Where is Picture-in-Picture? My ATEM had dedicated buttons for it, the Deck/Duet has none!
A25. For some reason Osee decided to make the PiP call a set of macros, so PiP is hidden in the macro keys: MEM1 is PiP top left, MEM2 is PiP top right, MEM3 is PiP bottom left, MEM4 is PiP bottom right, MEM5 is PiP off. Alternatively you can set up your own PiP (one) and use it with the upstream key (KEY + ON AIR buttons), but then you’ll have to switch between keying types as needed in the menu. Also, that will prevent you from using the logo with Luma Key or Chroma Key, only DSK (see Q21). In Duet 8 ISO there are no default macros, you have to create your PiP yourself — or better use MultiSource (SuperSource) with one source (Window 1) enabled.
Starting firmware 2.3.1 for Deck and Duet, MEM buttons work in  Easy Mode by default. Toggling MEM 1, 2, 3, 4 buttons enables and disables corresponding PiP from IN1. Easy Mode actions cannot be changed.

Q25a. What is Easy Mode for MEM buttons?
A25a. Starting firmware 2.3.1 for Deck and Duet a new feature was introduced, called Easy Mode. It is a default state. If you want to switch back to using macros (some of them have to be rewritten because coordinate measurement method was changed for consistency), go to Menu → Macro → button function and change Easy Mode to Macro Mode.
Easy Mode settings are the following: 
1..4: Corresponding PiPs from In 1 (1 is top left, 2 is top right, 3 is bottom left, 4 is bottom right).
5: Still 1 in DSK with Pre-multiplied Key
6: Still 2 in DSK with Pre-multiplied Key
7: Small Chroma Key window from In 1 in the bottom right
8: Zoom-Out MultiSource In1 and In2 — but it completely turns off MultiSource (you have to turn it back on again in the menu; I object and hope that this will be fixed later).


Q26. I want my own macros! 
A26. Well, you can do your own macros. You have three vacant buttons to play with, MEM6, MEM7, MEM8. It is unwise to overwrite PiP unless you are absolutely sure about what you are doing. Press and hold the MEM button of your choice for 2 seconds until it blinks. Punch buttons, choose menu entries… Just do your thing. Once done, short press the blinking button. If you did everything right, it will light up and will stay that way forth. Firmware 2.1.x: you can store and recall up to 100 macros. Create and store them in software, recall either in software or in Bitfocus Companion, upload and export using the software. You don’t need to overwrite the original PiP macros stored under MEM1..MEM5 buttons. 
While writing your own macros from scratch in a text editor, pay attention to ChArAcTeR CaSe, even if case sensitivity is removed starting firmware 2.1.3! UNIX-like systems (and GSD runs some kind of an embedded Linux system for ARM SoC) are unforgiving and even vile when it comes to messing up character case, quotes, spaces, semicolons, brackets, dashes, and all those seemingly unimportant little things! It is not "in 1" but always "In 1". Not "aux" but "Aux". Not "mic 1" but "Mic 1". At that, the capitalization in the macro engine itself is not consistent, so pay attention.
Quotes are extremely important as well: ONLY "this". Not «this», not even “this”.  Mind spaces as well. Linux is NOT Windows! "In 1", "in 1", "In1" and "in1" are four absolutely different entities, and in the GoStream realm only the first one exists. Other three lead to the void of a sudden bug-out and reboot — even when you are live and/or recording.
By the way, I highly recommend using a syntax-highlighting text editor for working with structured files like GoStream’s (and ATEM’s) XML. For Windows, it’s Notepad++. For Mac, CotEditor. For Linux, Notepadqq (inspired by Notepad++).  Full table of macro commands and parameters is here.
Do not switch macros too often. I recommend once in at least 5 seconds or less often.
If you want to add one or more macros to your existing set while not touching other settings, you must include all your previously uploaded macros into the new file. So, if you have a Duet and want to use macros from both Q27 and Q43, you have to prepare a file that includes all macros between <MacroPool> and </MacroPool>, and necessarily keep in mind the numbering of macro slots, so that two macros won’t get into the same slot, this will make the device bug out on you.
One more thing to remember is that if you want something to be disabled while something else is enabled, you must explicitly disable that something. See how long is each macro in Q27, for example. Macros are not an AI, they just dutifully repeat your button presses and menu selections exactly in the order you press or select them, so if you want to switch from PiP or MultiSource to another MultiSource layout, you absolutely have to disable PiP and completely reiterate everything you want to see: positioning of sources, masks and patterns, border colors, keying, video and sound inputs. GSD is a relatively simple machine after all, and cannot think for you. What is enabled is enabled, what is disabled is disabled — and what is not, remains in its original state.

Q27. Can I export my macros? Can I generally backup and restore my GoStream settings?
A27. Macros are exported to SD card or SSD into profiles folder when you export the profile (thus making a backup of the mixer configuration; to import macros or settings you have to place the relevant .xml file into profiles folder) in Menu → Setting → Profile → Export. With firmware 2.1.x the same can be done directly to your computer using PC/Mac software (File → Save). 
When you click on Profile.xml under Export, it exports the file with the current timestamp in the name, so you won't overwrite your previously exported profiles. Then you scroll the file down, look for <MacroPool>. After scrolling down further, through PiPs assigned to buttons 1 to 5, you'll find your macros assigned to 6, 7, and 8. 
Profiles store a snapshot of your current configuration, including all macros, streaming keys, currently enabled RTMP destinations (including custom ones if they are enabled), configuration of inputs (except HDMI out 2 in Deck/Duet that always reverts to Multiview), outputs, audio levels and delays, what's enabled and disabled, names of images in the internal memory (but not these images) if they are loaded from the SD card or SSD.
Importing a complete profile after a factory reset restores everything except still images. Importing any part of a profile restores/overwrites only that part of settings.
Here is an example of what can be punched in and assigned to buttons 6 and 8 to switch between the sides of MultiSource. Button 7 makes a MultiSource with the HDMI 1 on the left to a speaker, HDMI4 on the right for big slides, and HDMI2 as a lower right PiP to show either an audience or a remote speaker. In the 7, if you replace “In 2” with “Aux”, you'll get your Aux there, be it a USB camera, a video from your storage media, or an NDI|HX source.
Avoid switching between these macros more frequently than once in 10..15 seconds during live streaming with the mixer itself and recording into it at the same time. From my experience, that may lead to sudden unpredictable reboot with stopping a stream and recording, recovery to full operation may take up to 20 seconds. If your stream is set to automatically stop upon losing the source on YouTube, Facebook, or VK, you may fail to continue it. For versions before 2.1.0 Final, change “Zoom out” to “Zoom in”, but better update the firmware first.
Save the below monospace text as macro-ssrc.xml and load it using File → Load in PC/Mac software. If you have other macros (and you do, because you have PiP), append what’s below between <MacroPool> and </MacroPool>.
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Profile majorVersion="2" minorVersion="1" patchVersion="0" product="GoStream Deck">
<MacroPool>
<Macro index="6" name=”1+4 Slides Right" description="MultiSource with Speaker IN1 left, Slides IN4 right, Still1 BG">
            <Op id="keyOnAir" onAir="false"/>
<Op id="pgmIndex" input="black"/>
            <Op id="superSourceSource1" source1="In 1"/>
            <Op id="superSourceSource2" source2="In 4"/>
            <Op id="superSourceBackground" background="Still 1"/>
            <Op id="superSourceControlYPosition" YPosition="50"/>
            <Op id="superSourceControlStyle" style="Crop-Zoom out"/>
            <Op id="superSourceBorderWidth" borderIndex="0" width="1"/>
            <Op id="superSourceBorderColorHue" borderIndex="0" hue="200"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorSaturation" borderIndex="0" saturation="100"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorBrightness" borderIndex="0" brightness="100"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorHue" borderIndex="1" hue="200"/>
       <Op id="superSourceBorderColorSaturation" borderIndex="1" saturation="100"/>
          <Op id="superSourceBorderColorBrightness" borderIndex="1" brightness="0"/>
        </Macro>
        <Macro index="7" name="PiP2 BR, 1+4 Slides" description="SuperSource raised, with speaker IN1 left, Slides IN4 right, PiP2 on the bottom right, Still1 BG">
            <Op id="pgmIndex" input="black"/>
            <Op id="superSourceSource1" source1="In 1"/>
            <Op id="superSourceSource2" source2="In 4"/>
            <Op id="superSourceControlYPosition" YPosition="0"/>
      <Op id="superSourceControlStyle" style="Crop-Zoom out"/>
            <Op id="keySourceFill" key="PIP" fill="In 2"/>
            <Op id="keyOnAir" onAir="true"/>
            <Op id="keyYPosition" key="PIP" yPosition="6.8"/>
      <Op id="superSourceBorderWidth" borderIndex="0" width="1"/>
            <Op id="superSourceBorderColorHue" borderIndex="0" hue="200"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorSaturation" borderIndex="0" saturation="100"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorBrightness" borderIndex="0" brightness="100"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorHue" borderIndex="1" hue="200"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorSaturation" borderIndex="1" saturation="100"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorBrightness" borderIndex="1" brightness="0"/>
        </Macro>
        <Macro index="8" name="4+1 Slides Left" description="SuperSource with Slides IN4 left, Speaker IN1 right, Still1 BG">
          <Op id="keyOnAir" onAir="false"/>
<Op id="pgmIndex" input="black"/>
            <Op id="superSourceSource1" source1="In 4"/>
            <Op id="superSourceSource2" source2="In 1"/>
            <Op id="superSourceBackground" background="Still 1"/>
            <Op id="superSourceControlYPosition" YPosition="50"/>
            <Op id="superSourceControlStyle" style="Zoom out-Crop"/>
            <Op id="superSourceBorderWidth" borderIndex="0" width="1"/>
            <Op id="superSourceBorderColorHue" borderIndex="0" hue="200"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorSaturation" borderIndex="0" saturation="100"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorBrightness" borderIndex="0" brightness="100"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorHue" borderIndex="1" hue="200"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorSaturation" borderIndex="1" saturation="100"/>
<Op id="superSourceBorderColorBrightness" borderIndex="1" brightness="0"/>
 </Macro>
</MacroPool>
</Profile>
Profile.xml also contains your streaming URLs and streaming keys, so be careful if you decide to send it to some other GSD user, do cleanups beforehand.
Profile.xml's syntax, like Streaming.xml's, suspiciously resembles that of ATEM, although in terms of macros it is different. Won't say that either of that is bad.
If you lost the original Deck/Duet PiP macros and want to restore them where they belong, download this XML file and import as a profile or using File → Load in the software. If you want to place them into slots other than 1 to 5, change digits in Macro index="N" before uploading to the switcher.


Q28. Can I use a USB webcam as an input, play a video from the SD/SSD, and use an NDI|HX source simultaneously?
A28. Not in the Deck/Duet. Either a USB video source, OR file playback, OR NDI|HX input. Quick switching between sub-sources of Aux using macros is possible with the Deck firmware 2.1.x. If you need a webcam hooked to your Deck, a video file playback through it, and NDI|HX input all at once, you have to use a PC with OBS or vMix and two extra HDMI outs, with two inputs on the Deck reserved for that.
In Duet 8 ISO, for one video player you have to have inputs 5 or 6 assigned to Player, and inputs 7 or 8  assigned to UVC. That way you’ll have one player, one webcam and two NDI or SRT inputs, and four physical inputs. At that, with Duet 8 ISO you have to abandon either a PC connection or Iso recording because there are only two USB ports. For 60p workflow, keep Duet 8 ISO in Mode 0 (first four inputs HDMI or SDI).


Q29. SRT SUPPORT? Can GoStream Deck or Duet work with SRT (Secure Reliable Transport) protocol? Will it ever be able to do SRT?
A29. No SRT support in Deck and Duet, but maybe it will appear. No precise time frame, but this matter is being actively researched. As of March 2026, only RTMP/RTMPS output and a NDI HX input are supported by the Deck/Duet and Duet 8 ISO (a one-time-for-lifetime purchase for the Deck). Duet 8 ISO supports up to 4 SRT inputs, SRT outputs will be implemented later.


Q30. MY MONITOR DOESN’T WORK! I bought a cool new and slick (portable) monitor, it works with everything, displays everything — except the Deck/Duet. The Deck/Duet works with everything else except this new monitor. What the heck?
A30. Check your output settings. Highly likely that you have the Deck set to something not 1080p60 that is a long-standing standard for LCD and OLED monitors. It is also highly likely that your desired output has lower frame rate than your new shiny HDR, 120 (144) Hz capable monitor understands, meaning it can't process broadcasting and cinematic frame rates. In this case your options are to get a scaler-converter, or use a dedicated field production monitor. Which, alas, is not as slim and costs more, at least for the 14..16-inch diagonal you want. Zeuslap P16 is confirmed to work at 23.98p and higher (set its aspect ratio to 16:9); Zeuslap P16K too — but it is not optimal because its resolution is higher than 1080p. 
There is a 14-inch 1080p Osee display from the Deck Kit sold on AliExpress separately  (here it is in the official store; also here or here — pay attention while choosing the item, and the latter two are NOT Osee official stores!). 
The following models are also known to support 25/30p input, verified by GoStream users: Zeuslap P16 (16” slim), AT156 (15.6” slim, may not support 25p, only 30p), Benq EW2770QZ (27” desktop), ViewSonic VX2458-MHD (24” desktop).
Another way to solve this problem is to get a signal converter. For example, the LccKaa SCART/HDMI to HDMI scaler/converter (AliExpress, Amazon). As an added bonus, this box gives you analog or digital (coaxial) audio output at line level and, of course, can convert an analog video signal to HDMI. As well, you can use Blackmagic UpDownCross 3G or Avmatrix SC2030.
As for the Deck output, it cannot, at least yet, be different for various outputs. If you set 1080p60, or 1080p25, it will send exactly that at once to both HDMI outs, Ethernet/RTMP streaming, and USB/UVC output — but UVC will remain within 1080p30 regardless. So you have a tough choice between your new monitor and proper 6 Mbps streaming.
Another reminder: USB monitors will not work with GoStream because there is a fundamental protocol incompatibility. GoStream outputs UVC, monitors accept only DisplayPort Extensions. 


Q31. Can it stream 1080/60p? If so, is it true 60p, or just 30p with duplicated frames?
A31. The Deck/Duet, if its output is set to 1080p60 in Menu → Setting → Out format, and it gets 60p input, indeed streams true and honest 60p, except USB1 that is practically 30p with half of frames dropped.
That was tested and confirmed by streaming to YouTube that is known to fully support 60p and 50p. A 60 fps file was played from the SD card, output was set at 1080p60 — and it indeed streamed at true 60 fps with the Deck’s own streaming engine.
Remember that if you set output to 60p, but your inputs are lower (30, 29.97, 25, 24, 23.98), you'll get frame duplication and frame rate conversion from at least those sources. With other high frame rates (50 and 59.94) the conversion probably happens too, but won’t be as noticeable.
So feed 1080p60 to all ports (and over NDI|HX if you use it), use only 1080p60 files for playback, and you'll get a true 60p stream. Also, if you necessarily want to output 60p to a computer, grab the OUT1 HDMI to a USB 3.0 capture dongle, use a splitter if necessary.


Q32. Can I have a different frame rate for the monitor and for streaming/recording? For example, I want to stream at 1080p25 or 1080p30, but the monitor I’d like to use only supports 1080p60
A32. Sadly, no. Hardware limitations. The output setting is one for HDMI 1 and 2 outputs, USB 1 output, recording, and streaming. So either stream at a higher frame rate, or (better) seek another monitor or get a converter box, as per Q30. For hard reset, see Q40. Duet 8 ISO has a dedicated HDMI output 3 for Multiview that supports only 60p or 50p. Multiview there is exclusive to that HDMI output and can't be routed to USB or other outputs. If you need more instances of Multiview, use a splitter.


Q33. 720P or 4K OUTPUT? Can I stream at 720p or 1080i using the GoStream Deck/Duet internal capabilities? Can I stream 4K? I see only 1080p in the output settings… I also want to have a “virtual zoom” (resize) on inputs and outputs…
A33. Sadly (or not), no. 4K is not allowed by the hardware. The Deck and Duet can only output 1080p at all standard frame rates between 23.98 and 60. If you need to stream at 720p that bad, your only sensible option is to downscale the Deck’s output in OBS (or vMix) and stream using a computer, or stream using external devices like Zowiebox. What you see in Streaming.xml regarding 720p streaming doesn’t matter, as this file seems to be directly cloned from ATEM mixers. Scaling the image (“virtual zoom”) is not possible either, and unlikely will be. There is a China+Vietnam+Indonesia-only version of the Deck (not Duet) called Deck 4K that supports 4K30 all around, but it is a one-off thing, only one batch is manufactured and it is not officially sold outside these markets. When this batch runs out, there will be no such option outside the second-hand market.
So if a connection on location is bad, I can only advise improving it using cellular bonding. I have a good recipe for a kit of several 4G modems and Raspberry Pi that can be battery-powered or run on mains power. It isn’t as hard or complicated to build on your own as it seems, although indeed requires some reading, understanding, and copy-pasting. DM me if you have questions, the advice is free.


Q34. SPLITTERS? Can I use splitters to duplicate HDMI OUT 1 and HDMI OUT 2? I bought some cheap and simple ones but they don't work
A34. Remember: ANY video signal splitter, switcher, extender, or amplifier MUST be actively powered. Usually the extra power required is 5V, often over USB, or 12V with more serious devices — but still it’s an extra power. A passive splitter, particularly HDMI splitter, is a flop by definition. Maybe an Ethernet RJ-45 1M×2F works, but at the cost of a network cable length reduction.


Q35. MY PC HAS NO HDMI! I need to feed a computer output into the Deck but the computer in  question does not have an HDMI output. What should I do?
A35. This answer is relevant for any HDMI mixer/switcher. You can use the following cables and adapters, depending on the output of your computer:
a. DisplayPort to HDMI — either a cable with DP (male) to HDMI (male);
b. mini DisplayPort (looks similar to Thunderbolt 1/2/3 that happens on older Macs) (male) to HDMI (female), then normal HDMI cable, or miniDP (male) to HDMI (male) cable;
c. DVI-D (male) to HDMI (female), then a normal HDMI cable;
d. mini VGA2HDMI active upscaling converter, set the output to 1080p. Use normal VGA 15-pin D-Sub (male to male) display cable, and a normal HDMI cable.
e. If your computer is a newer Mac, or a Windows/Linux machine that can output display signal over USB-C, use Ugreen 25772 (CM725) capture, plugging the “device” end into computer and “computer” end into USB2 of the GoStream, select UVC (Webcam) for Aux.


Q36.CABLES! I see that HDMI cables have different versions. Which one is good enough for use with the Deck?
A36. Practically it’s any HDMI cable you can find. 1080p60 without HDR support works with HDMI 1.3 for displays, and that is the earliest version of an HDMI cable one can sensibly find in stock online and offline in 2025. The newer the better, anyway — and your HDMI cables normally should be version 1.4a and above, as they have Ethernet and 3D support, and you never know what you can encounter being a mobile broadcaster on demand. So 1.4a, 2.0, 2.1 — anything will do.


Q37. CABLES! How long an HDMI cable can be with the Deck to have it all hassle-free? 
A37. HDMI is a box of trickery, if you ask me (even if we don’t have things like HDCP and EDID in the scheme, let’s not go there). Normally you should use regular copper HDMI cables only if they are 7 metres (21 ft) or shorter. Sometimes thick copper cables as long as 10 m/33 ft work, but that is a lottery. Very thick infrastructure-grade copper HDMI cables by Kramer sometimes work at length of 15 metres (45 ft) or even 20 metres (66 ft), but that is a lottery too. One can often find such cables going to projectors at venues or institutions.
You have several options of solving the long distance problem:
At distances between 10 metres (33 ft) and 150 metres (450 ft) and if it is safe for the cable, you can use fiber optical HDMI. Optical fiber is a tender creature, avoid sharp angles and people stomping on it, use floor cable covers or at least thick tape. Be gentle while rolling and unrolling these cables because hard hits may do internal damage to connectors, and with fiber optical HDMI connectors are very important. Also remember, that fiber optical HDMI cables are unidirectional.
Another option for such distances is to use SDI cables. For our purpose, 3G-SDI is more than enough. That implies additional converters for HDMI devices. Use field-grade SDI, soft enough to bend but hard enough to survive an occasional stomp. You can safely use SDI cables up to 110 m (330ft) at least, sometimes up to 150 m (450 ft) depending on cable brand and type (3G-SDI).
You can use HDMI network extenders but only with computers (ideally only PowerPoint, PDF, or ProPresenter, but with Zoom and other video conferences that sort of works too) and not with cameras. If you use a HDbit-T or a HDbase-T Ethernet extender with a camera, you most surely will run into synchronization issues, delays, and problems with sound.
Wireless senders. Don’t look at anything cheap unless you are absolutely sure of what you are doing. Anything under $250 for a new TX+RX kit is an utter junk, full of bugs and/or unacceptable latency. Also, divide the advertised work distance of wireless video senders by two or even three for total reliability, and try to use them within line of sight only. Two exceptions are VNNBBS (Vrriis) and Shimbol TP Max  senders, they are cheap and, at line of sight, very reliable. Recommended wireless systems are Accsoon CineView SE and Cineview 2 SDI (CineView HE less so).
NDI|HX. If you have only one source where a possible occasional small delay doesn’t create any problems, GoStream Deck can receive just one NDI|HX signal at a time over a local network. If your source (a camera) does not have the NDI|HX support, I advise using a hardware encoder. I use LinkPi ENC1-V3 (no endorsement). For NDI-related matters see Q15. Zowietek Zowiebox HDMI or SDI to NDI HX is recommended by the community and myself.
Q38. Can the Deck/Duet display four video sources in the Preview or Program at once as a classic surveillance quadrator, i.e. in 2×2 matrix? 
A38. No. The closest to that you can get is if you set MultiSource to Zoom mode, move it vertically to the top or to the bottom, enable PiP and play with its size and position. You will then see full (but scaled) sources in the foreground and one in the background. If you can place the important part of the shot of the fourth source into one of the upper or lower corners of the background, good for you. 
Remember that PiP is incompatible with other types of Upstream Key (Chroma and Luma), because the Deck has only one user-accessible upstream keyer and one user-accessible downstream keyer. So if you use the trick with the logo display (see Q21), enabling PiP will hide the logo. Similarly, if you are shooting on a green/blue screen and are using the Chroma Key function, PiP also will be disabled.
You can achieve something like that if you use two GoStream devices, the second being a Deck/Duet, an  M2 (the recommended thing) or even an China-only GoStream X3). Use MultiSource in Zoom out mode with inputs 2 and 3 of GSD, move those to the top or bottom of the screen. Use PiP at 0.5 scale (the biggest, ¼ of the screen) for input 1. As a background source, use input 4 to which you connect the GoStream M2. Plug in your fourth camera into input 1 or 2 of the M2, and make said input a PiP of max size, which is also ¼ of the screen). Position the M2 PiP so that it would be visible. Voilà, you have 4 sources in 2×2 pattern — and even an Aux left for overlays using DSK. Or just get Duet 8 ISO.


Q39. I have a number of NDI|HX-capable cameras or other sources. Can I see their previews all at once instead of HDMI inputs?
A39. No. The Deck/Duet can display only one NDI input at a time, and that is via the Aux in NDI mode. The assignment of inputs is purely hardware and cannot be altered. Also, the Osee agreement with NewTek implies no concurrent NDI sources. If you have an NDI-based system, and the cameras are NDI HX, a better option for you would be to purchase Duet 8 ISO (if you have four or less such cameras), or vMix (or obtain it otherwise if you are sure that you aren’t to face whatever legal consequences). vMix is an extremely powerful Windows-only software that in terms of sheer capabilities, if not ergonomics, makes literally all hardware or hybrid live production switchers hold a beer. If you are familiar with OBS but not with vMix, it is safe to say that OBS is very inconvenient and dysfunctional in comparison, although for some things and simple setups it is definitely better, particularly if you are into gaming and cyber sports streaming, for which OBS suits much better. vMix, like the majority of specialized professional commercial software, is overcomplicated, as well as has some intentional (or less so) omissions. With all due respect it is possible to say that a PC with two 4-port BMD DeckLink cards and a vMix is eons mightier and more flexible than any 8-port hardware mixer.

Q40. FULL RESET! How to factory reset (hard reset, master reset, hardware reset) the Deck/Duet without the menu? 
A40. If you need to reset the Deck/Duet without seeing the menu (for example, your multiview monitor doesn’t understand the frame rate lower than 60p or 50p, or anything except 60p, you have to push the left (volume) knob and hold it for 5 seconds. That will completely reset the Deck to the original factory state (preserving the firmware of course, and the NDI license if there is one already installed) — and to 1080p60 output. Remove the SD card and/or unplug the SSD before resetting to avoid possible data loss.


Q41. TALLY LIGHTS! Is there a tally light support?
A41. Software internet-based tally system that supports the updated control protocol (Deck/Duet f/w 2.3.0 and later, and Duet 8 ISO) is available at tallyflow.gabrieleserpi.com. Anything with a browser required. The price is reasonable for capabilities. More hardware-based open-source systems are in the works.
As of March 2026, there are four systems. 1) watch the manufacturer’s video here. A 2.4GHz system with four lights and a controller, a dual-band system (2.4GHz + 450 MHz, better for longer distances). Ask smartmediavisual.pro for details. Ships from Indonesia globally. Also, a (paid) app that turns your smartphone into a tally light

training_sources.title: Content Calendar Ryan Smolarz
training_sources.content:
Day,Times,Copy,Approved,Scheduled 
"Monday, December 1",,"Owning your medical office isn’t just about pride — it’s strategy.
✅ You control your rent
✅ You control your environment
✅ You build wealth every month through equity
Inflation works for you instead of against you.
#DoctorBusiness #RealEstateOwnership #FinancialIndependence",,HP
"Tuesday, December 2",,"In medicine, you wouldn’t operate without training. In investing, the same rule applies. Learn the fundamentals, shadow experienced investors, and only deploy capital when you understand the anatomy of a deal. #EducatedInvesting #PhysicianFinance",,HP
"Wednesday, December 3",,"Measuring only against ‘what’s next’ is a losing game. The real power comes when you turn around and ask, ‘What have I already built?’ That backward look hooks you to purpose, grounds you in progress, and stops you chasing an infinite horizon.",,HP
"Thursday, December 4",,"Most doctors stare at their P&L and think they’re looking at “math.”
They’re not.
Every number is a process.
Fix the process, and the number fixes itself.
That’s what a real CFO does—translate data into decisions.",,HP
"Friday, December 5",,"The greatest trap for physicians:
A practice that collapses if you take one day off.
Build systems. Build processes. Build a business that runs without you.
Otherwise, there is no exit—only exhaustion.",,HP
"Saturday, December 6",,"100% bonus depreciation can mean serious after-tax income for physicians investing in real estate this year.
Don’t miss one of the most powerful tax shelters available to professionals.
#TaxStrategy #PhysicianFinance #RealEstateTaxBenefits",,HP
"Sunday, December 7",,"Every great portfolio starts with one thoughtful move. Research, partner with experts, and commit to continuous learning. Small, smart steps today build the foundation for lasting wealth. #PhysicianFinance #WealthBuilding",,HP
"Monday, December 8",,"You cannot become wealthy if you’re a servant to debt.
Live below your means early.
Buy used. Avoid predatory equipment financing.
Discipline beats income.",,HP
"Monday, December 8",,"Doctors don’t need more income. They need a framework.

Freedom for Doctors shows physicians how to escape the trap of overwork, burnout, and financial confusion—and finally build autonomy.
If you’ve ever felt “stuck” despite a high salary, this book was written for you.

https://a.co/d/cnFtiS9",,HP
"Tuesday, December 9",,"Commingling business and personal spending destroys:
• Your liability protection
• Your valuation
• Your ability to sell
Clean books = real value.",,HP
"Wednesday, December 10",,"On this week’s episode, I sit down with Jorge Vazquez—CEO of Graystone Investment Group and one of the most experienced real estate investors in the game.

With 20+ years in real estate and 3,500+ deals closed, Jorge has been featured in Forbes, highlighted by the Tampa Bay Business Journal, and built Graystone into a top Florida investment group serving everyday and international investors.

From Subject-To and BRRRR to Airbnb and private lending, he’s mastered the creative strategies that actually move the needle—and now teaches them through Property Profit Academy.

He started as a financial advisor, saw how real wealth is built, and made the leap. Today he leads 65+ agents, five companies, and a mission to help ordinary people become extraordinary investors.

Full episode out now.",,HP
"Thursday, December 11",,"Your medical degree didn’t come with a wealth manual. This book fills the gap.

Physicians learn anatomy, pathology, and surgical precision…
But no one teaches us how to actually build wealth.
Freedom for Doctors changes that.",,HP
"Thursday, December 11",,"If you own extra space in your building, don’t let it sit vacant for years.
Professional landlords offer:

Tenant improvement packages

Broker commissions

Market exposure
Follow their lead, fill your space, grow your wealth.
#PassiveIncome #CRE #PhysicianInvestors",,HP
"Friday, December 12",,"Real estate gives doctors what medicine often cannot—control over income, time, and financial direction. By turning your earnings into assets, you can practice because you want to, not because you have to. #FinancialFreedom #MedicineAndMoney",,HP
"Saturday, December 13",,"If your P&L has 200 line items, you don’t have clarity.
You have chaos.
One bank account. One credit card. One clean chart of accounts.
Simplicity gives you visibility—and visibility gives you power.",,HP
"Sunday, December 14",,"Burnout isn’t just emotional. It’s financial.

When money controls your decisions, you lose your platform to advocate for your patients.
This book shows doctors how to reclaim their independence—through financial clarity.",,HP
"Monday, December 15",,"Most specialties—especially ENT—aren’t producing enough new physicians.
If the perfect partner doesn’t exist, grow one.
Mentor someone from your region, invest early, and build your successor before you need them.",,HP
"Tuesday, December 16",,"“The only way you lose is if you stop playing the game.”
Setbacks aren’t failure—they’re reps.
Every investor, every doctor, every leader gets knocked down.
What separates the successful from everyone else is simple:
They get up. They adjust. They keep going.
Your momentum comes from consistency, not perfection.
Keep playing the game.",,HP
"Wednesday, December 17",,"Wealth is not a mystery. It’s a skill.

If you can handle complex medical cases, you can absolutely learn the financial game.
Freedom for Doctors breaks it down step by step.",,HP
"Thursday, December 18",,A good broker and solid structure can turn a complicated deal into a predictable income stream. The right team will see what you miss and protect what you’ve built. Success in real estate is a team sport. #RealEstateStrategy #InvestorTeam,,HP
"Friday, December 19",,"When you train your staff, hit record.
Turn the transcript into:
• A summary
• A SOP
• A quiz
• A training module
One conversation becomes permanent leverage.",,HP
"Saturday, December 20",,"Doctors everywhere are asking the same question: “Why am I working harder and getting less freedom?”

The answer is in your financial structure, not your clinical skill.
This book shows you how to fix it.",,HP
"Sunday, December 21",,"Start small — your own practice space.
Then, leverage that success into new properties, syndications, and long-term investments.
Each asset becomes a stepping stone to freedom.
That’s asset stacking.
#WealthBuilding #DoctorsWhoInvest #FinancialFreedom",,HP
"Monday, December 22",,"In medicine, risk = bad.
In investing, risk = potential.
The key is learning how to manage, not avoid, it.
Because opportunity lives just beyond your comfort zone.
#InvestorMindset #PhysicianWealth #MoneyMedicine",,HP
"Tuesday, December 23",,"If you’re not learning AI now, you won’t be able to catch up in a year.
Forward-thinking physicians are already using AI for:
• Meeting summaries
• SOP creation
• Coding support
• Patient education
This is the new stethoscope.",,HP
"Wednesday, December 24",,"The system is designed to keep physicians busy—not wealthy.

Freedom for Doctors teaches you how to shift from exhausted practitioner to empowered investor.
Your time is too valuable to trade forever.",,HP
"Wednesday, December 24",,"Ahead of the Christmas holiday, we’re resurfacing a past episode that continues to deliver meaningful insight and perspective. We’ll be highlighting this throwback tomorrow—be sure to check back and revisit a conversation that still holds relevance.

🎥 Watch here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tBH2cyRjbKk&t=60s",,HP
"Thursday, December 25",,"🎄 This season isn’t about gifts — it’s about clarity.
Clarity on what really matters: family, purpose, and freedom.
Here’s to closing the year strong and entering the next one with vision and conviction.

From all of us at The Medicine & Money Show,
Merry Christmas and may your investments—and your impact—compound in every area of life.
#MerryChristmas #PurposeDrivenWealth #DoctorsWhoInvest #MedicineAndMoney",,HP
"Thursday, December 25",,"In observance of the Christmas holiday, we’re sharing a throwback episode that remains as relevant today as when it first aired. If you missed it—or want to revisit the insights—now is a perfect time to watch.

▶️ https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tBH2cyRjbKk&t=60s

Wishing you a peaceful and reflective holiday season.",,HP
"Friday, December 26",,"“Financial freedom isn’t about having more money. It’s about having the power to make decisions without fear.”
When you’re financially trapped, you say yes when you should say no.
You tolerate systems that don’t serve you.
You lose your voice.
But when you build freedom?
You practice better medicine. You make clearer decisions. You live on your own terms.
Money isn’t the goal—control is.",,HP
"Saturday, December 27",,"COVID exposed something brutal:
Many practices were 90 days from bankruptcy.
If you can’t survive one disruption, you don’t have a business.
Build reserves. Build redundancy. Build resilience.",,HP
"Sunday, December 28",,"Wealth isn’t random—it’s the result of structure, systems, and execution. Learn the rules, use data, and surround yourself with experts who sharpen your strategy. That’s how doctors become investors. #MedicineAndMoney #PhysicianInvestors",,HP
"Monday, December 29",,"If you want real autonomy in medicine, it starts outside the exam room.

Control your finances.
Control your career.
Control your life.
The book shows you how.",,HP
"Tuesday, December 30",,"Overspend personally, and your employer owns you.
Overspend in your practice, and debt owns you.
Either way, your decision-making is compromised.
Financial independence is an ethical advantage.
It protects your patients.",,HP
"Wednesday, December 31",,"Financial independence is not optional for physicians—it’s protection.

When you’re financially strong, you can push back against bad policies, insurance limitations, and burnout culture.
This book gives you that foundation.",,HP

training_sources.title: VIRTUAL ASSISTANT STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)
training_sources.content:
VIRTUAL ASSISTANT STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)
Speaking Engagements & TED Talk Applications


OVERVIEW
This SOP outlines the process for researching, applying to, and tracking speaking engagements. The focus is on two main areas: (1) TEDx Talk applications, and (2) Physician-focused conference speaking opportunities.


Core Message for TEDx: “The Medical System is Failing You and This is Why” - How doctors without financial affairs in order are dependent on the system, and why patients should seek doctors with sound financial strategies.


Core Message for Physician Conferences: The LIFTOFF NOW Framework - A 10-step process to help physicians shift from consumer to investor mindset.


—


SECTION 1: TEDX TALK APPLICATION PROCESS


STEP 1: Research & Identify TEDx Events
1.1 Go to TEDx.com and filter for events based on these criteria:
* Location: United States, Caribbean, Canada, and other English-speaking countries
*    - Application Deadline: Within the next 6 months
*    - Language: English-speaking events only
*    
* 1.2 For each event found, record the following in the tracking spreadsheet:
*    - Event Name
*    - Location (City, Country)
*    - Application Deadline Date
*    - Event Date
*    - Event Theme/Focus
*    - Contact Email
*    - Application Link/URL
*    - Status: “Identified”
STEP 2: Review Event Alignment
2.1 Assess whether the event theme aligns with the talk: “The Medical System is Failing You and This is Why”
* Look for themes around health, healthcare disruption, financial literacy, wellness, or system change
*    - If alignment is unclear, note this in the spreadsheet but still proceed with application
STEP 3: Draft Application Materials
3.1 Customize the TEDx talk proposal for each event:
* Write a compelling speaker bio that highlights healthcare and financial expertise
*    - Draft a 200-300 word talk description/abstract tailored to the event’s theme
*    - Include personal story elements that resonate with the core message
*    - Ensure the pitch emphasizes why doctors’ financial independence matters
*    On FRIDAY - email to Ryan the items/questions in the application link that needs to be answered by him.
* 3.2 Save all draft materials with naming convention: “[Event Name][Date]DRAFT”
3.3 Update spreadsheet status to: “Draft Completed”


STEP 4: Submit Draft to Founder for Review
4.1 Send draft application materials to founder via email with subject line:
   “TEDx [Event Name] - Application Draft for Review”
-    On FRIDAY - email to Ryan the items/questions in the application link that needs to be answered by him.
   
4.2 Include in email:
* Event name, location, and application deadline
*    - All draft materials (bio, talk description, any other required fields)
*    - Link to the event/application portal
*    
* 4.3 Turnaround expectation: Founder will review and provide feedback within 3-5 business days
4.4 Update spreadsheet status to: “Awaiting Review”


STEP 5: Revise Based on Feedback
5.1 Once founder provides feedback, incorporate all requested changes
5.2 Resubmit revised draft to founder for approval (if needed) or proceed to submission


STEP 6: Submit Application
6.1 Once approved by founder, submit the application through the official TEDx portal
6.2 Keep a copy of the submitted application saved locally
6.3 Note submission date and confirmation number (if provided)
6.4 Update spreadsheet status to: “Submitted” and record submission date


STEP 7: Follow-Up & Tracking
7.1 Set a calendar reminder for 2 weeks before the application deadline to follow up with event organizers
7.2 Send follow-up email inquiring about application status
7.3 Update spreadsheet with follow-up date and any response received
7.4 Continue to follow up until you receive a response (accepted, declined, or pending)
7.5 Update final status in spreadsheet: “Accepted,” “Declined,” or “Pending”


—


SECTION 2: PHYSICIAN-FOCUSED CONFERENCE SPEAKING OPPORTUNITIES


STEP 1: Research & Identify Physician Conferences
1.1 Research conferences where physicians gather to discuss finance, investing, entrepreneurship, and career growth
   
1.2 Primary conferences to track:
* White Coat Investor Annual Conference (1 main annual event + smaller webinars)
*    - Physician Wellness and Financial Literacy Conference
*    - Passive Income MD Summit
*    - FinCon (tracks geared toward physicians)
*    - Financial Freedom Through Real Estate Conference
*    - American Association of Physician Leadership Events
*    - Physician Side Gigs Events
*    - Leverage & Growth Summit
*    - Wealthy Doc Events
*    - Physician on FIRE Community Events
1.3 For each conference, record:
* Conference Name
*    - Location
*    - Typical Date (if recurring)
*    - Speaker Application Deadline
*    - Speaker Fee/Honorarium Info (if available)
*    - Contact Email
*    - Application Link
*    - Status: “Identified”
STEP 2: Assess Paid vs. Unpaid Opportunities
2.1 Note whether the conference offers:
* Paid speaking fees/honorariums
*    - Travel/accommodation coverage
*    - Exposure only
*    
* 2.2 Prioritize conferences that offer compensation, but also include high-profile unpaid opportunities that build credibility
* 2.3 Mark in spreadsheet: “Paid,” “Unpaid,” or “Negotiable”
STEP 3: Draft Conference Proposal
3.1 Customize proposal for each conference based on the LIFTOFF NOW Framework:
* Write a compelling speaker bio emphasizing physician financial expertise
*    - Draft a 150-250 word session description that highlights the 10-step process
*    - Include learning outcomes (what physicians will gain from your session)
*    - Tailor to the conference’s audience and focus areas (finance, entrepreneurship, wellness, etc.)
3.2 Save all drafts with naming convention: “[Conference Name]_[Year]_DRAFT”


3.3 Update spreadsheet status to: “Draft Completed”


STEP 4: Submit Draft to Founder for Review
4.1 Send draft proposal materials to founder via email with subject line:
   “[Conference Name] - Speaker Proposal Draft for Review”
   
4.2 Include in email:
* Conference name, location, and deadline
*    - All draft materials (bio, session description, learning outcomes)
*    - Link to application portal
*    - Honorarium/payment information (if applicable)
*    
* 4.3 Turnaround expectation: Founder will review within 3-5 business days
4.4 Update spreadsheet status to: “Awaiting Review”


STEP 5: Finalize & Submit
5.1 Incorporate founder feedback
5.2 Once approved, submit application through official portal
5.3 Keep copy of submitted proposal
5.4 Update spreadsheet status to: “Submitted” with submission date


STEP 6: Follow-Up & Tracking
6.1 Set calendar reminders to follow up 1 week before deadline and again 2 weeks after submission
6.2 Track all responses (accepted, declined, waitlisted, pending)
6.3 Update spreadsheet with final outcome and any relevant notes


—


SECTION 3: TRACKING & METRICS SPREADSHEET


Create a Google Sheet with the following columns:


COLUMN HEADERS:
* Event/Conference Name
* - Event Type (TEDx / Physician Conference)
* - Location
* - Application Deadline
* - Event Date
* - Draft Completed Date
* - Submitted Date
* - Follow-Up Due Date
* - Status (Identified / Draft in Progress / Awaiting Review / Submitted / Pending Response / Accepted / Declined)
* - Notes/Outcome
METRICS TO TRACK (Calculated in separate summary section):
* Total Events/Conferences Identified (Month/Quarter)
* - Total Applications Drafted
* - Total Applications Submitted
* - Pending Responses
* - Accepted Engagements
* - Declined/Rejected Engagements
* - Awaiting Review
* - Honorarium/Payment Total (for physician conferences)
—


SECTION 4: REPORTING & UPDATES


4.1 Weekly Status Update
* Send founder a brief weekly email every Friday with:
*       Number of new events identified this week
*      • Number of drafts completed
*      • Number of applications submitted
*      • Current pending applications (with status)
*      • Any acceptances or rejections received
*    - Include a link to the tracking spreadsheet for detailed review
4.2 Monthly Progress Report
* Send comprehensive monthly report (1st of each month) including:
*      • Month-over-month comparison of applications submitted
*      • Overall acceptance/decline rate
*      • Upcoming deadlines (next 60 days)
*      • Recommended adjustments to strategy (if any)
*      • Highlights of any acceptances or paid opportunities
4.3 Spreadsheet Access
* Maintain a shared Google Sheet accessible to both VA and founder
*    - Update in real-time as each step is completed
*    - Founder can review progress anytime
—


SECTION 5: QUALITY STANDARDS & BEST PRACTICES


5.1 Application Quality
* All drafts must be customized to each event (no generic templates)
*    - Ensure alignment between the event’s theme and the core message
*    - Proofread all materials for grammar and formatting before submission
*    - Ensure all required fields are completed
5.2 Timeline Management
* Submit draft to founder at least 7-10 days before application deadline
*    - Allow 3-5 days for founder review and feedback
*    - Complete revisions within 2-3 business days of receiving feedback
*    - Submit final application at least 2-3 days before deadline (not last minute)
5.3 Communication
* All draft submissions must include relevant event details and context
*    - Respond to founder feedback promptly
*    - Flag any events with accelerated timelines or tight deadlines
*    - Proactively communicate if you anticipate missing any deadlines
—


SECTION 6: EXPANSION (Future Phase)


Once TEDx applications are rolling and acceptances begin coming in, transition focus to:
* Researching additional physician-focused speaking platforms
* - Identifying niche medical association events
* - Tracking emerging physician entrepreneurship conferences
* - Building a pipeline of 6-12 month speaking opportunities
—


END OF SOP


























































STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)
MedMoney Incubator / MedInvest Platform Monitoring, Engagement, and Outreach
Document Version: 1.0
Owner: MedMoney Incubator
Role Responsible: Business Assistant (BA) / Virtual Assistant (VA)
________________


1. PURPOSE
This SOP defines the daily, weekly, and monthly responsibilities of the Business Assistant (BA) responsible for monitoring, engaging, and growing the MedMoney Incubator / MedInvest platform. The goal is to ensure platform health, consistent engagement, steady physician onboarding, and measurable outreach performance.
________________


2. SCOPE
This SOP covers:
* Platform monitoring (site, feed, admin, bugs)
* Community engagement and replies
* Physician outreach (LinkedIn)
* Social media posting and monitoring
* Verification and document review
* Metrics tracking and reporting
* Advertiser outreach (milestone-based)
________________


3. TOOLS & ACCESS REQUIRED
The BA must have access to:
* MedMoney Incubator / MedInvest Admin Dashboard
* OPMED document submission area
* LinkedIn (personal or company-approved account)
* Facebook (Medicine & Money Show Page)
* Instagram
* TikTok
* YouTube Studio (Shorts visibility only)
* Google Sheets (metrics tracking)
* Approved outreach message templates
* Platform-generated referral / signup links
________________


4. DAILY RESPONSIBILITIES
4.1 Platform Feed Monitoring & Engagement
Frequency: Daily (minimum once per business day)
* Review all new posts on the MedInvest platform feed
* Monitor comments and discussions
* Reply to posts and comments using approved brand voice
* Flag any of the following for escalation:
   * Medical misinformation
   * Inappropriate content
   * Compliance concerns
   * Legal or reputational risk
________________


4.2 New Sign-Ups & Verification Monitoring
* Check daily site sign-ups
* Review Admin → Verification section
* Identify users who submitted medical licenses
* Confirm submissions are complete
* Flag incomplete or questionable submissions
________________


4.3 OPMED Document Submissions
* Check daily for any documents submitted to OPMED
* Verify documents are present and properly uploaded
* Flag missing or incomplete submissions
* Report daily status in internal notes
________________


4.4 Bug & Site Health Monitoring
* Review Admin → Bugs / Issues section daily
* Click through key site pages to confirm:
   * No broken links
   * Pages load correctly
   * Signup and login flows work
* Immediately report any broken links or bugs
________________


5. DAILY OUTREACH RESPONSIBILITIES
5.1 LinkedIn Physician Outreach
Daily Target: 20 physicians per day
Steps:
1. Identify physicians on LinkedIn (MD/DO)
2. Send personalized, compliant outreach messages
3. Include custom platform referral link
4. Track:
   * Messages sent
   * Responses
   * Conversions (if visible)
Rules:
* Messages must be professional and value-based
* No spam or automation that violates LinkedIn policy
* Always include opt-in language
________________


6. SOCIAL MEDIA MANAGEMENT
6.1 Daily Posting
Platforms:
* Facebook (Medicine & Money Show Page)
* Instagram
* LinkedIn
* TikTok
* DailyPost (if applicable)
Requirements:
* Minimum 1 post per day
* All posts must link back to:
   * MedMoney Incubator / MedInvest platform
* Ensure branding and messaging consistency
________________


6.2 Social Monitoring & Replies
* Monitor comments on all posts published
* Reply to comments using brand-approved responses
* Flag sensitive or complex inquiries for escalation
________________


6.3 YouTube Shorts Verification
* Confirm YouTube Shorts are:
   * Successfully uploaded
   * Visible
   * Playing correctly
* Flag any missing or failed uploads
________________


7. FACEBOOK PAGE MONITORING
Medicine & Money Show Facebook Page
* Monitor comments and messages daily
* Reply to engagement where appropriate
* Ensure page reflects latest content
* Report any issues or unusual activity
________________


8. METRICS & REPORTING
8.1 Daily Internal Tracking
The BA must track the following metrics daily:
* New site sign-ups
* Medical license verifications submitted
* OPMED documents submitted
* LinkedIn outreach messages sent
* Social posts published
* Comments replied to
* Bugs identified
________________


8.2 Monthly Metrics Report (Google Sheets)
The BA must maintain a shared Google Sheets file with the following columns:
Recommended Sheet Structure:
* Month
* Total Site Members
* New Members Added
* Medical License Submissions
* OPMED Documents Submitted
* LinkedIn Outreach Messages Sent
* Email Outreach Sent
* Social Posts Published
* Total Comments
* Comments Replied To
* Bugs Identified
* Bugs Resolved
Frequency: Updated continuously; reviewed monthly
________________


9. MONTHLY METRICS MEETING SUPPORT
* Prepare metrics summary prior to monthly meeting
* Ensure Google Sheet is up to date
* Highlight trends:
   * Growth rate
   * Engagement changes
   * Outreach effectiveness
* Be prepared to answer basic performance questions
________________


10. ADVERTISER OUTREACH (MILESTONE-BASED)
10.1 Trigger Condition
* Begins once platform reaches 2,000 members
________________


10.2 Advertiser Outreach Tasks
* Use existing advertiser list to send outreach emails
* Track responses and interest
* Re-contact past advertiser prospects
* Actively build a new advertiser prospect list
* Maintain separate advertiser outreach tracking
________________


11. ESCALATION RULES
Escalate immediately if you encounter:
* Legal or compliance issues
* Platform outages
* Data privacy concerns
* Aggressive or inappropriate user behavior
* Public-facing errors or broken links
________________


12. PERFORMANCE EXPECTATIONS
* Consistent daily execution
* Clear, organized reporting
* Proactive identification of issues
* Professional communication at all times
* Measurable outreach activity
________________


13. CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT
This SOP will be reviewed and updated as:
* Platform features evolve
* Outreach scales
* Monetization initiatives expand














1. LinkedIn Outreach SOP (100 Invites per Week)
Objective


Send 100 connection requests per week to doctors to expand the network and create future podcast guest and community opportunities.


Daily Target


20 connection requests per day (Monday–Friday)


Step-by-Step Procedure
Step 1: Find Doctors


Use LinkedIn search and filters:


Search examples:


“Physician”


“Medical Doctor”


“Surgeon”


“ENT”


“Cardiologist”


“MD”


Confirm profile shows:


MD or DO (or approved equivalent)


Currently practicing or involved in medicine


Log in tracking sheet:


Name


Profile link


Specialty


Status: Ready to Invite


Step 2: Send Connection Request


Open profile
Click: Connect → Send


Optional connection note (if allowed):


Example:
“Hi Dr. [Last Name], I help coordinate outreach for a physician podcast and investment community. Your work stood out—would value connecting.”


Log in tracking sheet:


Date


Invite sent


Status: Pending


Step 3: After They Accept


Send welcome message within 24 hours:


Template:
“Thanks for connecting, Dr. [Last Name]. We run a physician podcast and investment network. Are you open to being a podcast guest or connecting further?”


Log:


Accepted


Message sent


Step 4: Follow-Up Once Only


If no reply after 5 days:


“Just follow up in case you missed this. Would love to connect further if relevant.”


If no reply after follow-up:
Mark: No Response and stop messaging


Weekly Maintenance


Once per week:


Withdraw pending invites older than 3 weeks.


Path:
My Network → Invitations → Sent → Withdraw


KPI Targets


Weekly goals:


100 invites sent


20–40 acceptances


5–15 conversations started


1–5 podcast guest opportunities


2. Podcast Outreach SOP
Objective


Contact podcast hosts to recommend approved guests and secure podcast appearances.


Step-by-Step Procedure
Step 1: Find Podcasts


Search:


Google:
“medical podcast”
“doctor podcast”
“physician podcast”


Spotify:
Search medical specialties


YouTube:
Search medical podcast interviews


Log:


Podcast name


Host name


Email/contact link


Step 2: Send Outreach Email


Template:


Subject: Guest suggestion for your podcast


Email:


Hi [Host Name],


I help coordinate guests for a physician podcast and network.


Dr. [Guest Name] would be a strong guest for your audience. They have valuable insights on [topic].


Would you be open to discussing a potential appearance?


Best regards
[VA Name]


Log status:
Pitch Sent


Step 3: Follow-Up


Follow-up after 5 days if no reply:


“Just following up in case this got buried.”


Only send one follow-up.


KPI Targets


Weekly:


20 podcast pitches


3–10 replies


1–3 bookings


3. Social Media Comment Monitoring SOP
Objective


Monitor and respond to comments on YouTube, LinkedIn, and other social platforms.


Platforms to Monitor


Primary:


YouTube


LinkedIn


Secondary (if assigned):


Instagram


X (Twitter)


Facebook


Check Comments 3 Times Per Day


Morning
Midday
End of day


Comment Response Rules
Positive comment


Reply:
“Thank you, appreciate you watching/listening.”


Question (general)


Reply with helpful answer.


Example:
“Great question. We cover that topic in this episode: [link]”


Negative comment


Reply professionally:
“Thanks for the feedback. We appreciate your perspective.”


Do NOT argue.


Spam or abusive comment


Delete or ignore.


Log if severe.


Escalate Immediately If:


Legal threats


Harassment


Media inquiries


KPI Targets


Daily:


100% of comments reviewed


Respond to all legitimate comments


4. LinkedIn Recommendation SOP
Objective


Post LinkedIn recommendations for podcast guests to strengthen relationships.


Step-by-Step Procedure
Step 1: Open Guest LinkedIn Profile


Click:
More → Recommend


Step 2: Write Recommendation


Template:


“I had the pleasure of hosting Dr. [Name] on our podcast.


They were extremely knowledgeable, clear, and provided valuable insights.


Highly recommend Dr. [Last Name] to any organization or audience.”


Step 3: Submit Recommendation


Log in tracking sheet:


Guest name


Date posted


Status: Completed


KPI Targets


Weekly:


2–5 recommendations posted


Tracking Sheet Structure (Required)


Columns:


Date
VA Name
Task Type
Platform
Name
Profile Link
Action Taken
Status
Next Follow-Up Date
Notes


Daily VA Schedule Example


Daily total time: 2–4 hours


LinkedIn outreach:
60 minutes


Podcast outreach:
45 minutes


Social monitoring:
60 minutes


Recommendations:
15 minutes


Tracking and logging:
15 minutes


Weekly Targets Summary




LinkedIn invites: 100 - 20
Podcast pitches: 20  - 4
Comment monitoring: daily
Recommendations: 2–5 - 1

scores.question: A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 die. How many sheep are left?
scores.response:
If the farmer originally had 17 sheep and all but 9 of them died, then the number of sheep left would be 9. 

To break it down simply: "All but 9" means that 9 sheep survived while the rest did not.

<properties>
{"Category":"health","Created time":"2026-03-10T18:00:25.517Z","Duration (seconds)":503.608046,"Overview":"The content captures portions of an ENT clinic day, including documentation and a patient encounter. Clinicians discuss prior options for sleep-related breathing issues (possible sleep study, CPAP, oral appliance) and nasal exam findings (no adenoids, reduced turbinates, no pus or polyps). There’s also an administrative issue about a prescription not reaching the pharmacy and the need to resend it, possibly to Walgreens per patient’s request. The main focused encounter involves a patient complaining of a sensation of water or fullness in the ear without recent flying or illness. Examination suggests Eustachian tube dysfunction: the tube connecting the ear and nose isn’t equalizing pressure properly, causing fullness and “water” sounds despite no visible middle ear fluid. The clinician explains the anatomy and function of the Eustachian tube, tests the ear by asking the patient to perform a Valsalva maneuver (pinch nose and blow), and plans hearing and Eustachian tube function testing to further evaluate.","Speakers":5,"Title":"ENT visit for ear “water” sensation and Eustachian tube dysfunction","url":"https://www.notion.so/31f5596730a081beb8c8d2510eb6ac17"}
</properties>
